- **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda W$ — verified across all steady-state queues.
- **Utilization:** $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ — should be **< 0.85** for a stable system.
- **M/M/1 formulas:** $L = \rho/(1-\rho)$, $W = 1/(\mu - \lambda)$, $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho)$.
- **Blocking probability** in M/M/1/K: $P_K = (1-\rho)\rho^K / (1-\rho^{K+1})$.
- **Confidence interval:** $\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2, n-1} \cdot S/\sqrt{n}$.
- **Bottleneck** = machine with utilization > 85% AND persistent queue.
- **Distributions to know cold:** Exponential, Triangular, Normal, Lognormal, Weibull, Erlang, Poisson.
- **30 replications** is the KTU-standard default for ±5% precision.
- **Warm-up period** removes initial-condition bias; 10% rule of thumb.
- **Tool-specific jargon translation:**
  - Entity (Arena, ProModel) = Object (Simio) = Part (Witness)
  - Create = Source = Arrival = Create
  - Process = Server = Service = Machine
  - Dispose = Sink = Exit = Ship
- **Practical viva question:** "How would you validate your simulation model?" → Answer: compare output statistics (mean cycle time, utilization) against real plant data using **two-sample t-test** at 95% confidence.
- **Practical viva question:** "What is the difference between verification and validation?" → Verification = *"Are we building the model right?"* Validation = *"Are we building the right model?"*
- **Practical viva question:** "Why use triangular distribution when data is sparse?" → It only needs min, mode, max — the expert-estimate approach for incomplete data.
- **Practical viva question:** "Why is Lognormal common for machining times?" → Because machining times are **right-skewed** (long tail of difficult-to-cut parts), which Lognormal models naturally.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of healthcare systems using Simio/Arena/Promodel/Witness
topics.content:
> **APJ ADJUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIEL607 - MODELLING AND SIMULATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Simulation of healthcare systems using Simio/Arena/Promodel/Witness ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Healthcare System Simulation** is the application of **Discrete Event Simulation (DES)** to model, analyse, and optimise the operational behaviour of healthcare delivery systems — such as emergency departments, outpatient clinics, inpatient wards, surgical theatres, and diagnostic laboratories — by representing patients, resources, processes, and decision rules as interacting logical entities whose state changes occur at discrete instants in time. In KTU terminology, this experiment falls under **"Application-Oriented Simulation Modelling"**, where the student is expected to map a real-world service system onto a software tool, validate it, and statistically interpret Key Performance Indicators (KPIs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Favourite)** > *Healthcare simulation is the computer-based imitation of patient flow through clinical pathways, used to quantify system performance under varying demand, staffing, and policy scenarios — without disturbing the real hospital.* ### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Hospital as a Factory" Intuition Imagine a hospital as a **car assembly plant**: - **Patients** are the cars entering the production line. - **Doctors, nurses, MRI machines, beds** are the workstations. - **Waiting rooms** are the buffers (storage areas). - **Triage, diagnosis, treatment, discharge** are the assembly stations. If too many cars arrive and the stations are slow, a **queue (buffer overflow)** forms — exactly what happens in the Emergency Department. A simulation software lets you "fast-forward time" inside this virtual plant, test "what-if" policies (e.g., add one more doctor during peak hours), and observe the effect on waiting time and congestion **without touching a real patient**. > [!NOTE] > **Healthcare-specific KPIs** (must memorise for ESE): > - **Average Waiting Time (AWT)** — minutes > - **Length of Stay (LOS)** — hours/days > - **Bed Occupancy Rate (BOR)** — % (typical target: **85%**) > - **Server/Resource Utilisation (ρ)** — fraction (0 to 1) > - **Patient Throughput** — patients/day ### 1.3 Why Simulation and Not Just Queueing Formulas? | Method | Limitation in Healthcare | |---|---| | Pure queueing theory (M/M/c) | Assumes exponential, time-stationary, single queue | | Spreadsheet / static analysis | Cannot capture stochastic arrivals and complex routing | | Direct hospital experimentation | **Unethical, expensive, risky to patients** | | **DES with Arena/Simio/ProModel/Witness** | Handles **non-exponential**, **time-varying**, **priority**, **balking/reneging** behaviour realistically | > [!TIP] > **Examiner Hook**: When asked *why use simulation in healthcare?* always answer with three keywords: **safety (no patient risk), cost (cheaper than trial-and-error), and complexity (handles stochastic, multi-resource systems)**. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Building Blocks of Any Healthcare Simulation Model 1. **Entities** → Patients (sometimes also staff or specimen samples). 2. **Resources** → Doctors, nurses, rooms, equipment, beds. 3. **Processes (Modules)** → Triage → Consultation → Lab Test → Treatment → Discharge. 4. **Attributes** → Patient severity, age, arrival mode (walk-in/ambulance). 5. **Controls** → Routing rules, prioritisation, queue disciplines (FIFO/priority). ### 2.2 Distributions Most Commonly Fitted to Healthcare Data | Random Variable | Common Distribution | Justification | |---|---|---| | Inter-arrival time of outpatients | **Exponential** | Poisson arrivals | | Inter-arrival time of emergencies | **Exponential / Non-homogeneous Poisson** | Time-varying rate λ(t) | | Consultation time (GP) | **Lognormal** | Right-skewed service | | Length of stay (inpatient) | **Lognormal / Weibull** | Heavy-tailed, varies with diagnosis | | Surgery duration | **Triangular / Beta** | Bounded, expert-estimated | | Time to bed availability | **Gamma** | Erlang-k stages of cleaning/turnover | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table) | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Meaning | |---|---|---| | $\rho$ | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c \cdot \mu}$ | Utilisation of $c$ identical servers | | Little's Law | $L = \lambda W$ | Avg # in system = arrival rate × avg time | | $L_q$ (M/M/c) | $L_q = P_0 \, \dfrac{(c\rho)^c \, \rho}{c!\,(1-\rho)^2}$ | Avg # in queue | | $W_q$ (M/M/c) | $W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda}$ | Avg waiting time in queue | | $W$ | $W = W_q + \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Total time in system | | $P_0$ | $P_0 = \left[\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{c-1}\dfrac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \dfrac{(c\rho)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)}\right]^{-1}$ | Probability of empty system | | BOR | $\text{BOR} = \dfrac{\text{Bed-days occupied}}{\text{Bed-days available}} \times 100\%$ | Bed occupancy rate | | CV | $\text{CV} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu}$ | Coefficient of variation (use Kingman for G/G/1) | | Kingman | $W_q \approx \left(\dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho}\right)\left(\dfrac{C_a^2 + C_s^2}{2}\right)\dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Approx. wait in G/G/1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary condition for stability**: $\rho < 1$ strictly, i.e., $\lambda < c\mu$. If $\rho \ge 1$, the queue grows unbounded and the steady-state does **not exist**. This is the most common reason simulation runs diverge to infinity — always check first! ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Beyond hospitals, the same conceptual framework is applied to: - **Air-traffic control towers** (aircraft = patients, runways = servers). - **Manufacturing job-shops** and semiconductor wafer fabs. - **Call centres and BPO operations**. - **Disaster-evacuation planning** and pandemic surge modelling (post-COVID relevance). ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Performance of an M/M/c Queueing Model We consider an **M/M/c** queue representing, e.g., a multi-doctor OPD clinic where: - Patients arrive as a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$. - Each doctor serves with i.i.d. exponential service times of rate $\mu$. Let $P_n$ be the steady-state probability that there are $n$ patients in the system. The birth–death balance gives: $$ P_n = \begin{cases} \dfrac{(c\rho)^n}{n!}\,P_0, & 0 \le n < c \\[8pt] \dfrac{(c\rho)^n}{c!\,c^{\,n-c}}\,P_0, & n \ge c \end{cases} $$ **Step 1 — Derive $P_0$ using the normalisation condition** $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1$. $$ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1}\dfrac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} \, P_0 \;+\; \sum_{n=c}^{\infty}\dfrac{(c\rho)^n}{c!\,c^{\,n-c}}\,P_0 \;=\; 1 $$ The second sum is geometric with ratio $\rho$: $$ \sum_{n=c}^{\infty}\dfrac{(c\rho)^n}{c!\,c^{\,n-c}} = \dfrac{(c\rho)^c}{c!}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\rho^k = \dfrac{(c\rho)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)} $$ Hence: $$ P_0 = \left[\sum_{n=0}^{c-1}\dfrac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \dfrac{(c\rho)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)}\right]^{-1} $$ **Step 2 — Average number in queue $L_q$**: Only states $n \ge c$ contribute (the excess over $c$ is in queue). $$ \begin{aligned} L_q &= \sum_{n=c}^{\infty}(n-c)\,P_n \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k\,P_{c+k} \\ &= \dfrac{P_0\,(c\rho)^c}{c!}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k\,\rho^k \;=\; \dfrac{P_0\,(c\rho)^c}{c!}\cdot\dfrac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} \end{aligned} $$ So: $$ L_q = P_0\,\dfrac{(c\rho)^c\,\rho}{c!\,(1-\rho)^2} $$ **Step 3 — Average waiting time** $W_q$ via **Little's Law**: $$ W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda}, \qquad W = W_q + \dfrac{1}{\mu}, \qquad L = \lambda W $$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Question) **Problem:** A hospital Emergency Department has **3 doctors**. Patients arrive as a Poisson process at rate $\lambda = 10$/hr. Service time per doctor is exponential with mean **12 minutes**. **Step 1 — Convert units**: $\mu = 60/12 = 5$/hr per doctor. **Step 2 — Compute utilisation**: $$ \rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c\mu} = \dfrac{10}{3 \times 5} = \dfrac{10}{15} = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667 $$ Since $\rho < 1$, steady-state exists. ✅ **Step 3 — Compute $P_0$**: $$ \begin{aligned} P_0 &= \left[\sum_{n=0}^{2}\dfrac{(3\rho)^n}{n!} + \dfrac{(3\rho)^3}{3!\,(1-\rho)}\right]^{-1} \\ &= \left[\dfrac{2^0}{0!} + \dfrac{2^1}{1!} + \dfrac{2^2}{2!} + \dfrac{2^3}{6 \cdot \frac{1}{3}}\right]^{-1} \\ &= \left[1 + 2 + 2 + \dfrac{8}{2}\right]^{-1} \\ &= \left[1 + 2 + 2 + 4\right]^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{9} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute $L_q$**: $$ L_q = \dfrac{1}{9} \cdot \dfrac{8 \cdot \frac{2}{3}}{6 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2} = \dfrac{1}{9} \cdot \dfrac{16/3}{6/9} = \dfrac{1}{9} \cdot \dfrac{16}{3} \cdot \dfrac{9}{6} = \dfrac{16}{18} = \dfrac{8}{9} \approx 0.889 $$ **Step 5 — Compute waiting and total times**: $$ W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{8/9}{10} = \dfrac{4}{45}\,\text{hr} \approx 5.33\,\text{min} $$ $$ W = W_q + \dfrac{1}{\mu} = \dfrac{4}{45} + \dfrac{1}{5} = \dfrac{4}{45} + \dfrac{9}{45} = \dfrac{13}{45}\,\text{hr} \approx 17.33\,\text{min} $$ $$ L = \lambda W = 10 \times \dfrac{13}{45} = \dfrac{130}{45} = \dfrac{26}{9} \approx 2.889 \text{ patients} $$ --- ### 3.3 Summary of Computed Results | Performance Measure | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Utilisation per doctor | $\rho$ | $66.67\%$ | | Probability system is empty | $P_0$ | $11.11\%$ | | Average # in queue | $L_q$ | $0.889$ | | Average # in system | $L$ | $2.889$ | | Average waiting time in queue | $W_q$ | $5.33$ min | | Average total time in ED | $W$ | $17.33$ min | > [!TIP] > **Interpretation for the board**: With 3 doctors, only ~67% busy on average — a candidate for **re-allocation** to another ward. If we removed one doctor (c=2), utilisation would rise to 100% (unstable boundary) and queues would explode — a classic **bottleneck vs cost-trade-off** decision healthcare managers face daily. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Patient-Flow Diagram of a Generic Hospital (DES Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patient Arrival
Poisson lambda per hour] --> B[Triage Module
Resource Nurse 1] B --> C{Severity Classifier
Attribute Based} C -->|Critical| D[Emergency Bay
Resource Doctor 3] C -->|Moderate| E[Consultation Room
Resource Doctor 1 or 2] C -->|Minor| F[Fast Track
Resource Doctor 4] D --> G[Decision Module
Admit or Discharge] E --> H{Lab Test Required} F --> I[Discharge Process
Exit Entity] H -->|Yes| J[Lab Module
Resource Lab Tech] H -->|No| I J --> K[Treatment Decision] K --> G G -->|Admit| L[Inpatient Ward
Resource Bed] G -->|Discharge| I L --> M[Final Discharge
Exit Entity] ``` ### 4.2 Software-Tool Comparison Block Diagram (Feature Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DES["Discrete Event Simulation Tools"] T1["Arena by Rockwell"] T2["Simio by Simio LLC"] T3["ProModel by ProModel Corp"] T4["Witness by Lanner"] end subgraph Features["Core Capabilities"] F1["Drag Drop Modules"] F2["3D Animation"] F3["Object Orientation"] F4["Healthcare Templates"] F5["OptQuest Optimizer"] F6["External Code Interface"] end T1 --- F1 T1 --- F5 T2 --- F2 T2 --- F3 T2 --- F4 T3 --- F1 T3 --- F4 T4 --- F1 T4 --- F6 ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Capability Matrix (KTU Viva Reference) | Feature | Arena | Simio | ProModel | Witness | |---|---|---|---|---| | Learning curve | Moderate | Moderate–Steep | Easy | Easy | | 3D animation | Limited (basic) | **Native, strong** | Built-in | Built-in | | Healthcare library | Add-on | **Built-in templates** | **Built-in templates** | Custom-built | | Optimisation | **OptQuest** | Simio OptQuest | Built-in | Built-in | | Object-oriented paradigm | Process-based | **Fully OO** | Hybrid | Process-based | | Academic licence availability | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Best suited for | Beginners / KPI | Complex routing | Quick hospital models | Logistics + healthcare | ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define healthcare system simulation. List any three Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) commonly measured in such simulations.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Healthcare system simulation is the use of computer-based discrete event simulation to model patient flow, resource utilisation, and process performance in clinical settings (1 mark). Three KPIs: (i) Average waiting time of patients in queue (1 mark) (ii) Resource / server utilisation factor (1 mark) (iii) Bed occupancy rate or patient throughput per day (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State Little's Law. Why is it considered the most fundamental relationship in queueing-based healthcare simulation?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Little's Law: $L = \lambda W$, where $L$ is the long-run average number of customers in the system, $\lambda$ is the long-run average arrival rate, and $W$ is the average time spent in the system (2 marks). It is fundamental because it is **distribution-independent** — valid for any stochastic system in steady state, making it the universal validation check for any healthcare DES model (1 mark). --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step methodology** for developing a healthcare simulation model using **Arena Simulation Software**. Illustrate the major modules used (Create, Process, Decide, Assign, Record, Dispose). (7 marks) **(b)** A hospital outpatient clinic has **2 doctors**. Patients arrive in a Poisson stream at the rate of **$\lambda = 15$/hr**. Service time per doctor is **exponential with mean 10 minutes**. Calculate: (i) Server utilisation $\rho$, (ii) $P_0$, (iii) $L_q$, (iv) $W_q$, (v) Average number of patients in the clinic $L$. (7 marks) --- **Solution to Q.A(a) — Arena Methodology (7 marks)** 1. **Problem formulation** (1 mark): Define objectives — e.g., reduce average waiting time below 15 minutes. 2. **Data collection** (1 mark): Gather arrival rates (Poisson), service-time distributions (exponential/lognormal), number of doctors/nurses. 3. **Model building in Arena** (3 marks): - `Create` module generates patient entities with inter-arrival times. - `Process` module captures consultation with `Seize–Delay–Release` (doctor is resource). - `Decide` module branches patients based on severity attribute. - `Assign` module updates attributes (e.g., total cost incurred). - `Record` module captures statistics (waiting time, queue length). - `Dispose` module removes patients who exit. 4. **Verification & Validation** (1 mark): Check against Little's Law, historical KPIs. 5. **Experimentation & Output Analysis** (1 mark): Run multiple replications with different warm-up periods; report confidence intervals. --- **Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)** Given: $\lambda = 15$/hr, $\mu = 60/10 = 6$/hr, $c = 2$. **(i) Utilisation**: $$ \rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c\mu} = \dfrac{15}{2 \times 6} = \dfrac{15}{12} = 1.25 $$ $\rho > 1 \Rightarrow$ **system is unstable**, queue grows without bound. Steady-state does **not exist** (1 mark). **(ii)–(v) Numerical approach — simulation required**: Since analytical steady-state fails, we use **transient simulation** in Arena over a finite horizon (e.g., 8-hour shift). Expected behaviour from transient $M/M/2$ with $\rho = 1.25$: - $W_q$ grows roughly linearly after an initial transient — estimate via Arena `Record` module. - For approximate **Kingman-type G/G/c bound** with $C_a = C_s = 1$: $$ W_q \approx \left(\dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho}\right)\cdot 1 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\mu} = \dfrac{1.25}{-0.25} \cdot \dfrac{1}{6} \approx 0.833\,\text{hr} \approx 50\,\text{min} $$ - $L \approx \lambda W \approx 15 \times (50/60 + 10/60) \approx 15$ patients in clinic after a few hours. **Recommendation (1 mark)**: Add at least one more doctor so that $\rho < 0.85$. --- #### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]` **(a)** Compare **Simio, Arena, ProModel and Witness** as simulation platforms for healthcare applications with respect to **animation, object-orientation, optimisation support, healthcare templates, and learning curve**. (7 marks) **(b)** Design a discrete-event simulation model for a **diagnostic laboratory** with **3 technicians**. Sample- arrivals are Poisson at **$\lambda = 8$/hr**, processing time is **exponential with mean 15 minutes**. Run the model and compute steady-state performance for a typical 8-hour shift. (7 marks) --- **Solution to Q.B(a) — Software Comparison (7 marks)** Tabulate the four tools as in **Section 4.3** above, and elaborate as follows (1 mark each feature, 1 mark for synthesis): - **Animation**: Simio and Witness offer native 3D animation; Arena has only 2D; ProModel has decent 2D + 3D (1 mark). - **Object-orientation**: Simio is fully object-oriented (objects inherit properties); Arena/ProModel are process-modular; Witness is hybrid (1 mark). - **Optimisation**: All four have built-in optimisers; Arena uses OptQuest; Simio uses OptQuest; Witness has built-in (1 mark). - **Healthcare templates**: Simio and ProModel ship with dedicated healthcare modules (ED, OPD, OR); Arena and Witness need user-built modules (1 mark). - **Learning curve**: ProModel and Witness are easiest; Arena is standard for academics; Simio is steepest but most flexible (1 mark). - **Synthesis**: For a KTU lab experiment, **Arena** is recommended for first exposure; **Simio** for advanced 3D + OO work; **ProModel** for ready-made hospital templates (1 mark). --- **Solution to Q.B(b) — Diagnostic Lab Numerical (7 marks)** Given: $\lambda = 8$/hr, $\mu = 60/15 = 4$/hr, $c = 3$ technicians. **(i) Utilisation**: $$ \rho = \dfrac{8}{3 \times 4} = \dfrac{8}{12} = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667 \quad (\text{stable}) $$ **(ii) $P_0$**: $$ \begin{aligned} P_0 &= \left[1 + 2 + \dfrac{2^2}{2!} + \dfrac{2^3}{6 \cdot (1/3)}\right]^{-1} \\ &= \left[1 + 2 + 2 + \dfrac{8}{2}\right]^{-1} = \left[9\right]^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{9} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) $L_q$**: $$ L_q = \dfrac{1}{9}\cdot\dfrac{8 \cdot \frac{2}{3}}{6 \cdot \frac{1}{9}} = \dfrac{8}{9} \approx 0.889 $$ **(iv) $W_q$**: $$ W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{8/9}{8} = \dfrac{1}{9}\,\text{hr} \approx 6.67\,\text{min} $$ **(v) $W$ and $L$**: $$ W = \dfrac{1}{9} + \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{4 + 9}{36} = \dfrac{13}{36}\,\text{hr} \approx 21.67\,\text{min} $$ $$ L = 8 \times \dfrac{13}{36} = \dfrac{104}{36} = \dfrac{26}{9} \approx 2.889 $$ **Throughput per 8-hour shift** (1 mark): ≈ $8 \times 8 = 64$ samples (assuming stable arrivals, ignoring last queue spillover). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls** — Where Students Lose Marks: > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** — Arrival rate in /hr, service rate must also be /hr. Mixing minutes and hours costs **2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Not checking $\rho < 1$** — If utilisation $\ge 1$, the M/M/c analytical formulas **diverge**. Always state the stability condition first. > 3. **Confusing Little's Law** — Writing $L = \lambda/W$ instead of $L = \lambda W$ is a common sign-error; remember $L$ and $W$ are *proportional*. > 4. **Confusing tool features** — In comparison questions, students often write *“Simio is 2D”* or *“Arena has 3D”* — both wrong; check before writing. > 5. **Skipping warm-up period discussion** — In a simulation lab report, forgetting to mention **warm-up deletion** (deletion of initial transient) costs at least 1 mark. > 6. **Missing healthcare-specific KPIs** — Always quote at least one clinical metric (BOR, LOS, AWT) — generic queueing terms alone lose marks. --- ### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] Healthcare simulation = **DES applied to patient flow / resource allocation**. - [x] Building blocks: **Entities (patients)**, **Resources (staff, beds, equipment)**, **Processes (modules)**, **Attributes**, **Controls**. - [x] Always verify **$\rho = \lambda / (c\mu) < 1$** before applying steady-state formulas. - [x] Little's Law: $L = \lambda W$ — universally true, distribution-independent. - [x] M/M/c formulas: $P_0$, $L_q$, $W_q$, $W$, $L$ are board-favourites — derive $P_0$ from normalisation. - [x] Common distributions: Exponential (arrivals), Lognormal (consultation, LOS), Gamma (turnover), Triangular (surgery duration). - [x] Software map: - **Arena** → academic standard, process-modular, OptQuest optimiser. - **Simio** → fully object-oriented, strong 3D animation, healthcare templates. - **ProModel** → easy to learn, ready-made hospital modules. - **Witness** → flexible, hybrid, strong animation. - [x] Simulation methodology: **Formulate → Collect Data → Build Model → Verify → Validate → Experiment → Analyse → Implement**. - [x] KPIs to memorise: **AWT, LOS, BOR, Utilisation, Throughput**. - [x] Healthcare-specific: prioritisation rules (triage), balking/reneging, time-varying arrivals (peak hours). - [x] Validation tactic: Use **historical KPIs** + **Little's Law consistency check**. - [x] Always specify **run length**, **warm-up period**, **number of replications**, and **confidence intervals** in lab reports. - [x] Ethical note: Simulation is preferred over real-world experimentation in healthcare because **patient safety is non-negotiable**.

topics.title: Simulation of biological systems using Vensim/Powersim/Stella
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL607 - MODELLING AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of biological systems using Vensim/Powersim/Stella

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of System Dynamics Simulation

**System Dynamics (SD)** is a computer-aided approach to policy analysis and design that models the time-dependent behaviour of complex feedback systems. When applied to biological systems, it allows researchers to translate biological hypotheses (population growth, disease spread, predator–prey interactions) into mathematical models that can be simulated, tested, and visualised using dedicated software such as **Vensim**, **Powersim**, and **Stella (iThink)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the term **"Simulation of biological systems"** refers to constructing causal and stock-flow models in these graphical environments to study emergent behaviour over time without performing real-world experiments (which are often unethical, expensive, or infeasible in biology).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The three software tools listed (Vensim, Powersim, Stella) all belong to the same family — they are **iconographic, equation-based system-dynamics environments** where the user drags "stocks" (rectangles) and "flows" (valves/clouds) on a canvas, and the software automatically generates and solves the underlying differential equations.

### 1.2 Core Building Blocks of a System-Dynamics Model

Every biological SD model is composed of exactly four primitives:

| # | Primitive | Symbol in Stella/Vensim | Mathematical Role |
|---|-----------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Stock (Level)** | Rectangle | Accumulator — $\dfrac{dS}{dt}$ integral |
| 2 | **Flow (Rate)** | Double arrow / Valve | $\dfrac{dS}{dt}$ derivative |
| 3 | **Converter (Auxiliary)** | Circle | Algebraic function of other variables |
| 4 | **Connector** | Arrow | Information/cause–effect link |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stock** is what you *have* (e.g., number of rabbits, infected people, glucose concentration in blood). **Flow** is what *changes* the stock (birth, death, infection, metabolism). This distinction is the most heavily tested concept in KTU viva questions.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Biological Bathtub"

Imagine a **bathtub** filled with water. The **water level** in the tub is the *stock*. The **input tap** and the **drain** are *flows*. The **position of the taps** is controlled by an *auxiliary variable* (e.g., a thermostat = a converter).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Check:** A population of bacteria in a Petri dish is a "biological bathtub." Nutrient inflow + cell division = inflow rate. Cell death + nutrient depletion = outflow rate. The number of live cells is the stock. Stella/Vensim does this exact computation, but the icons (rectangles, valves) replace the visual imagery of taps and water.

### 1.4 Why Use Vensim / Powersim / Stella for Biology?

1. **Non-programming interface** — biologists (who often are not coders) can build models graphically.
2. **Automatic numerical integration** — Euler, Runge-Kutta 4, or RK45 solvers are built-in; the user does not code the integrator.
3. **Feedback visualisation** — positive/negative feedback loops (e.g., immune response vs. viral load) are explicitly drawn, aiding understanding.
4. **Scenario testing** — parameters (transmission rate $\beta$, recovery rate $\gamma$) can be swept in seconds.
5. **Reproducibility** — the model is a self-documenting graphical artefact acceptable in peer-reviewed journals.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse Stella with MATLAB/Simulink. Simulink is a **block-diagram signal-flow** environment, whereas Stella/Vensim is a **stock-flow causal** environment. They are NOT the same paradigm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Biological System Dynamics

A model built in Vensim/Powersim/Stella is mathematically equivalent to a system of first-order Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs). The software hides this complexity, but for KTU examinations you must understand what happens "under the hood."

#### Pillar 1 — Stocks are Integrals

A stock $S(t)$ is computed by the software as:

$$S(t) = S(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \text{Inflow}(\tau)\,d\tau - \int_{t_0}^{t} \text{Outflow}(\tau)\,d\tau$$

In discrete time (which is what the software actually executes internally with step $\Delta t$):

$$S(t+\Delta t) = S(t) + \big[\text{Inflow}(t) - \text{Outflow}(t)\big] \cdot \Delta t$$

This is **Euler's forward integration**. Stella/Vensim can be set to use **RK4 (Runge-Kutta 4th order)** for higher accuracy.

#### Pillar 2 — Flows are the Derivatives

A flow $F(t)$ in a biological model is almost always one of:
- A **constant** (e.g., immigration of 100 birds/year).
- A **function of stocks** (e.g., births = birth-rate × population).
- A **nonlinear function** (e.g., contact rate $= \beta S I$, as in epidemiology).

#### Pillar 3 — Feedback Loops Create Emergence

- **Negative feedback** → stability, equilibrium (e.g., predator–prey balance).
- **Positive feedback** → runaway growth or collapse (e.g., viral spread in an immunologically naive population).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table lists the **canonical models** that a KTU student is expected to recognise and be able to construct in Stella/Vensim.

| Model Name | Domain | Key Stocks | Differential Equations | Standard Parameters |
|------------|--------|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Malthusian (Exponential) Growth** | Bacteria in unlimited nutrient | $P$ (population) | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = rP$ | $r$ = intrinsic growth rate (per day) |
| **Logistic Growth** | Population with carrying capacity | $P$ | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = rP\!\left(1-\dfrac{P}{K}\right)$ | $r$, $K$ = carrying capacity |
| **Lotka-Volterra (Predator-Prey)** | Ecology | $X$ (prey), $Y$ (predator) | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \alpha X - \beta XY$    $\dfrac{dY}{dt} = \delta XY - \gamma Y$ | $\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta > 0$ |
| **SIR Epidemic** | Infectious disease | $S, I, R$ | $\dfrac{dS}{dt}=-\beta SI$, $\dfrac{dI}{dt}=\beta SI-\gamma I$, $\dfrac{dR}{dt}=\gamma I$ | $\beta$ = transmission, $\gamma$ = recovery |
| **SEIR Epidemic** | Disease with latent period | $S, E, I, R$ | Adds $\dfrac{dE}{dt}=\beta SI - \sigma E$ | $\sigma$ = inverse latent period |
| **One-Compartment PK** | Drug in bloodstream | $C$ (concentration) | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -kC + \dfrac{D}{V}\delta(t)$ | $k$ = elimination rate, $V$ = volume |
| **Cell-Glucose Dynamics** | Cellular metabolism | $G$ (glucose), $C$ (cells) | $\dfrac{dG}{dt}=-aGC$, $\dfrac{dC}{dt}=bGC$ | $a, b$ > 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions** for all biological stock models: $S(0), I(0), R(0) \ge 0$ and $S+I+R = N$ (closed population) — a standard KTU viva question.

### 2.3 Mapping the Formulae onto the Software Canvas

In **Stella**, the workflow is:
1. Drag a *Stock* onto the canvas; name it `P` (population).
2. Drag a *Flow* arrow into the stock; name it `births`.
3. Drag a *Flow* arrow out of the stock; name it `deaths`.
4. Drag *Converters* for the constants (`r`, `K`).
5. In the equation editor, type `births = r * P` and `deaths = r * P^2 / K`.
6. Click ▶ Run — the software solves the ODE numerically and plots $P(t)$.

In **Vensim**, the same model uses "Boxes" (stocks) and "Valves" (rates) connected by arrows, with equations typed in a separate formula bar.

In **Powersim**, the terminology is "Levels," "Rates," and "Auxiliaries," but the underlying mathematics is identical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** If the question asks for a "model description" of a biological system, you must always list **(i) stocks, (ii) flows, (iii) auxiliaries, and (iv) the governing ODE** — in that order. Marks are awarded in that exact sequence.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Application Domain | Engineering / Public-Health Utility |
|--------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Epidemiology | Predicting peak ICU demand during a COVID-19-like outbreak |
| Ecology | Sustainable fishing quotas to prevent predator–prey collapse |
| Pharmacology | Optimal dosing schedules to keep drug concentration in therapeutic window |
| Cell Biology | Designing bioreactors to maximise biomass yield |
| Conservation | Re-introduction strategies for endangered species |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive derivations** of the three most important biological models required by the KTU 2024 Scheme. No steps are skipped; every algebraic transition is explained.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Logistic Growth Equation

**Biological Premise:** A population of $P(t)$ individuals grows with intrinsic rate $r$, but environmental resistance (limited food, space) reduces the net growth rate linearly as $P$ approaches the carrying capacity $K$.

**Step 1 — Start with the Malthusian (exponential) model:**

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = rP$$

**Step 2 — Recognise that the *effective* growth rate must diminish as $P$ grows.** Define the effective rate $r_{\text{eff}}$ as:

$$r_{\text{eff}} = r\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $r_{\text{eff}}$ back into the Malthusian equation:**

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = r_{\text{eff}} \cdot P$$

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = r\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) P$$

**Step 4 — Expand for explicit use in the Stella equation editor:**

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = rP - \frac{rP^2}{K}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the equilibrium points** (where the population stops changing, $dP/dt = 0$):

$$0 = rP\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right)$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad P^* = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad P^* = K$$

**Step 6 — Stability analysis.** Take the derivative of the right-hand side $f(P) = rP - (r/K)P^2$:

$$f'(P) = r - \frac{2rP}{K}$$

Evaluate at $P^* = K$: $f'(K) = r - 2r = -r < 0$ → **stable equilibrium (carrying capacity).**
Evaluate at $P^* = 0$: $f'(0) = +r > 0$ → **unstable equilibrium (extinction).**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Implication:** If a student is asked to construct a Stella model of logistic growth, the **equation box for the outflow** (deaths) should read `r * P^2 / K`, and the **inflow** should be `r * P`. This single equation captures both the linear birth rate and the quadratic density-dependent death rate.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lotka-Volterra Predator-Prey Model

**Biological Premise:** In a closed ecosystem, prey $X(t)$ grow exponentially in the absence of predators but are consumed at a rate proportional to encounters. Predators $Y(t)$ die exponentially in the absence of prey but grow in proportion to successful predation.

**Step 1 — Prey equation in the absence of predators:**

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \alpha X, \quad \alpha > 0$$

**Step 2 — Predation term.** The encounter rate between predators and prey is bilinear (mass-action kinetics), with predation coefficient $\beta$:

$$\text{Predation rate} = \beta X Y$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the predation term from the prey growth equation:**

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \alpha X - \beta X Y$$

**Step 4 — Predator equation in the absence of prey** (exponential decay):

$$\frac{dY}{dt} = -\gamma Y, \quad \gamma > 0$$

**Step 5 — Predator growth term.** Successful predation converts prey biomass into new predators, with conversion efficiency $\delta$:

$$\frac{dY}{dt} = -\gamma Y + \delta X Y$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the coupled system:**

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \alpha X - \beta X Y$$

$$\frac{dY}{dt} = \delta X Y - \gamma Y$$

**Step 7 — Find non-trivial equilibrium $(X^*, Y^*)$** (excluding $X^*=0, Y^*=0$):

From the prey equation: $\alpha X^* = \beta X^* Y^* \Rightarrow Y^* = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}$

From the predator equation: $\delta X^* Y^* = \gamma Y^* \Rightarrow X^* = \dfrac{\gamma}{\delta}$

**Step 8 — Interpretation.** The system oscillates around $(X^*, Y^*)$ with **neutral stability** in the classical (un-damped) LV model. This is the famous "fox-and-rabbit" oscillation that students must be able to plot in Stella.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — SIR Epidemic Model

**Biological Premise:** A closed population of size $N$ is divided into Susceptible ($S$), Infected ($I$), and Recovered ($R$) individuals. Susceptibles become infected at rate $\beta SI$ (mass-action), and infecteds recover at rate $\gamma I$.

**Step 1 — Conservation law (closed population):**

$$S(t) + I(t) + R(t) = N = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2 — Rate of new infections** (force of infection). The probability of a susceptible meeting an infected per unit time is proportional to $I/N$. The number of such meetings is $\beta \dfrac{SI}{N}$. The number of *new infections per unit time* is therefore:

$$\text{New infections} = \beta \frac{S I}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Write the Susceptible differential equation:**

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \frac{S I}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Write the Infected differential equation** (inflow from $S$ minus outflow to $R$):

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \frac{S I}{N} - \gamma I$$

**Step 5 — Write the Recovered differential equation:**

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$$

**Step 6 — Add all three equations to verify conservation:**

$$\frac{dS}{dt} + \frac{dI}{dt} + \frac{dR}{dt} = -\beta \frac{SI}{N} + \beta \frac{SI}{N} - \gamma I + \gamma I = 0 \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$.** The dimensionless parameter that determines whether an epidemic occurs is:

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\gamma}$$

- If $R_0 > 1$: epidemic grows.
- If $R_0 < 1$: disease dies out.
- If $R_0 = 1$: threshold condition.

**Step 8 — Stella/Vensim implementation summary:**

| Variable | Type | Equation |
|----------|------|----------|
| $S$ | Stock | $S(t) - \beta \cdot S \cdot I / N$ |
| $I$ | Stock | $I(t) + \beta \cdot S \cdot I / N - \gamma \cdot I$ |
| $R$ | Stock | $R(t) + \gamma \cdot I$ |
| $N$ | Converter | $S + I + R$ |
| $\beta$ | Converter | numeric input |
| $\gamma$ | Converter | numeric input |
| $R_0$ | Converter | $\beta \cdot N / \gamma$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick verification step for KTU viva:** Set $S(0) = 999$, $I(0) = 1$, $R(0) = 0$, $\beta = 0.5$, $\gamma = 0.2$, $N = 1000$. Then $R_0 = 2.5 > 1$, and the model should produce a single infection peak before $I \to 0$.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Causal Loop Diagram (CLD) of Population Growth with Density Dependence

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Population P"]
    nodeB["Birth Rate"]
    nodeC["Death Rate"]
    nodeD["Carrying Capacity K"]
    nodeE["Density Stress"]
    nodeF["Net Growth dP/dt"]

    nodeA -- "+" --> nodeB
    nodeA -- "+" --> nodeC
    nodeB -- "+" --> nodeF
    nodeC -- "+" --> nodeF
    nodeF -- "+" --> nodeA
    nodeA -- "+" --> nodeE
    nodeE -- "+" --> nodeC
    nodeD -- "-" --> nodeE

    subgraph negFB["Negative Feedback Loop B1"]
        nodeA
        nodeC
        nodeE
        nodeD
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The loop **Population → Births → Net Growth → Population** is *positive* (more people → more births → more people).
- The loop **Population → Density Stress → Deaths → Net Growth → Population** is *negative* (more people → more crowding → more deaths → fewer people). This is the **stabilising loop** that produces the logistic S-curve.

### 4.2 Stock-Flow Diagram of the SIR Epidemic Model (Stella Style)

```mermaid
graph LR
    sStock["Stock S (Susceptible)"]
    iStock["Stock I (Infected)"]
    rStock["Stock R (Recovered)"]
    f1["Flow: New Infections"]
    f2["Flow: Recovery"]
    beta["Aux: beta Transmission"]
    gamma["Aux: gamma Recovery Rate"]
    nAux["Aux: N Total Population"]
    r0Aux["Aux: R0 Reproduction Number"]

    sStock -- "out" --> f1
    f1 -- "in" --> iStock
    iStock -- "out" --> f2
    f2 -- "in" --> rStock
    sStock -.-> f1
    iStock -.-> f1
    iStock -.-> f2
    sStock -.-> nAux
    iStock -.-> nAux
    rStock -.-> nAux
    beta -.-> f1
    gamma -.-> f2
    nAux -.-> r0Aux
    beta -.-> r0Aux
    gamma -.-> r0Aux
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Solid arrows** = flow of material (people moving from $S$ to $I$ to $R$). **Dashed arrows** = flow of information (used to compute rates). This is the **fundamental dichotomy** that KTU examiners test: stocks and flows move *people*, while connectors move *information*.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Stella/Vensim Model

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        P1["Parameter: beta"]
        P2["Parameter: gamma"]
        P3["Parameter: N"]
        P4["Initial Conditions S0, I0, R0"]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE["Computational Layer"]
        C1["Force of Infection: beta*S*I/N"]
        C2["Recovery: gamma*I"]
        C3["R0 = beta*N/gamma"]
        C4["Solver RK4 with dt = 0.01"]
    end

    subgraph STATE["State Layer"]
        S1["Stock S"]
        S2["Stock I"]
        S3["Stock R"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]
        O1["Time-series plot S vs t"]
        O2["Time-series plot I vs t"]
        O3["Phase plot I vs S"]
        O4["Sensitivity table R0"]
    end

    P1 --> C1
    P2 --> C2
    P3 --> C1
    P3 --> C3
    P1 --> C3
    P4 --> S1
    P4 --> S2
    P4 --> S3
    C1 --> S1
    C1 --> S2
    C2 --> S2
    C2 --> S3
    S1 --> C1
    S2 --> C1
    S2 --> C2
    S1 --> O1
    S2 --> O2
    S2 --> O3
    S1 --> O3
    C3 --> O4
    C4 --> S1
    C4 --> S2
    C4 --> S3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** When asked to "implement a biological model in Stella," examiners will check (1) that stocks have correct initial values, (2) that flows are typed as equations, and (3) that the **graphical output** matches a known analytical reference. Always include a screenshot of the plot in your lab record.

### 4.4 Causal Loop Diagram — Predator–Prey (Lotka-Volterra)

```mermaid
graph LR
    preyX["Prey X"]
    predY["Predator Y"]
    enc["Encounters beta*X*Y"]
    preyG["Prey Growth alpha*X"]
    predG["Predator Growth delta*X*Y"]
    predD["Predator Death gamma*Y"]

    preyX -- "+" --> preyG
    preyG -- "+" --> preyX
    preyX -- "+" --> enc
    predY -- "+" --> enc
    enc -- "-" --> preyX
    enc -- "+" --> predG
    predG -- "+" --> predY
    predY -- "+" --> predD
    predD -- "-" --> predY

    subgraph loopP["Positive Loop P - Prey"]
        preyX
        preyG
    end
    subgraph loopN["Negative Loop B - Encounters"]
        preyX
        enc
        predY
        predD
    end
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024]**
*Define the terms "Stock" and "Flow" as used in Stella/Vensim. Give one biological example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Stock** (also called a Level or Accumulator) is a quantity that accumulates over time and represents the *state* of the system at any instant. Mathematically, it is the integral of its net inflow. Biological example: number of infected individuals in a population at time $t$.

A **Flow** (also called a Rate) is the rate at which a stock changes per unit time. Biological example: the rate of new infections per day, equal to $\beta SI/N$.

> 1 mark for stock definition, 1 mark for flow definition, 1 mark for examples.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper]**
*What is the basic reproduction number $R_0$ in the SIR model? What is its significance for epidemic control?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$R_0$ is defined as $R_0 = \dfrac{\beta N}{\gamma}$, where $\beta$ is the transmission rate, $N$ is the total population, and $\gamma$ is the recovery rate. It represents the **average number of secondary infections** produced by one infected individual in a fully susceptible population.

Significance:
- If $R_0 > 1$, the epidemic **spreads**.
- If $R_0 < 1$, the epidemic **dies out**.
- Control strategies (vaccination, social distancing) aim to **reduce the effective reproduction number** below 1.

> 1 mark formula, 1 mark interpretation, 1 mark control implication.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*(a)* Derive the **logistic growth equation** for a population $P(t)$ with intrinsic growth rate $r$ and carrying capacity $K$. Identify the equilibria and discuss their stability. (7 marks)
*(b)* Construct a **Stella/Vensim stock-flow model** to simulate logistic growth. List the stocks, flows, auxiliaries, their equations, and the initial values you would use. Show a sketch of the expected $P(t)$ curve. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

*(a) Derivation (7 marks):*

Starting from the Malthusian equation $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = rP$, we modify the growth rate to account for environmental resistance proportional to population density:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = rP\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right)$$

Expanding: $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = rP - \dfrac{r}{K}P^2$ (1 mark)

Equilibria occur when $dP/dt = 0$:

$$rP\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; P^* = 0 \;\text{or}\; P^* = K \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Stability analysis using $f(P) = rP - (r/K)P^2$ and $f'(P) = r - (2r/K)P$ (1 mark):
- $f'(0) = +r > 0$ → $P^*=0$ is **unstable** (small populations grow away from zero). (1 mark)
- $f'(K) = -r < 0$ → $P^*=K$ is **stable** (perturbations return to $K$). (1 mark)

**Analytical solution (for the curve sketch):** Using separation of variables:

$$\int \frac{dP}{P(1 - P/K)} = \int r\,dt \;\Rightarrow\; P(t) = \frac{K}{1 + Ae^{-rt}}$$

where $A = (K - P_0)/P_0$. (2 marks)

*(b) Stella Model Construction (7 marks):*

| Element | Name | Type | Equation / Initial Value |
|---------|------|------|--------------------------|
| 1 | `Population` | Stock | $P(0) = 10$ |
| 2 | `Births` | Flow (in) | `r * Population` |
| 3 | `Deaths` | Flow (out) | `r * Population^2 / K` |
| 4 | `r` | Converter | `0.5` (per day) |
| 5 | `K` | Converter | `1000` (individuals) |

- Mark 1 for naming the stock.
- Mark 1 for the inflow equation.
- Mark 1 for the outflow equation.
- Mark 1 for parameter values.
- Mark 1 for solver choice (RK4, $dt = 0.01$).
- Mark 1 for the expected S-curve sketch: rapid initial growth followed by saturation at $P = K = 1000$. (1 mark)

**Expected curve:** Sigmoid (S-shape) from $(0, 10)$ rising monotonically and asymptoting at $P = 1000$ by approximately $t = 15$ time units.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam - July 2023 Pattern]**
*(a)* Set up the **SIR epidemic model** equations and explain the meaning of each term. Define the **basic reproduction number $R_0$** and state the threshold condition for an epidemic. (7 marks)
*(b)* Using **Vensim/Powersim**, describe the stock-flow diagram for the SIR model. What happens to $I(t)$ when (i) $R_0 < 1$ and (ii) $R_0 > 1$? Show the characteristic shape of the $I(t)$ curve for $R_0 = 2.5$ with $N = 1000$, $S(0) = 999$, $I(0) = 1$, $R(0) = 0$, $\beta = 0.0005$, $\gamma = 0.2$. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

*(a) SIR Equations (7 marks):*

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \frac{SI}{N} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \frac{SI}{N} - \gamma I \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Meaning of terms:** $S, I, R$ are stocks representing susceptible, infected, and recovered individuals (1 mark). $\beta$ is the transmission rate coefficient (per contact per day) (0.5 mark). $\gamma$ is the recovery rate (per day), so the mean infectious period is $1/\gamma$ days (0.5 mark). $N = S + I + R$ is the closed population (0.5 mark).

**Basic reproduction number:**

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\gamma} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Threshold condition:** An epidemic occurs if and only if $R_0 > 1$. (1 mark)

*(b) Vensim Model and Curve (7 marks):*

**Stocks:** `Susceptible`, `Infected`, `Recovered`. **Flows:** `New_Infections` (from $S$ to $I$), `Recovery` (from $I$ to $R$). **Auxiliaries:** `beta = 0.0005`, `gamma = 0.2`, `N = Susceptible + Infected + Recovered`, `R0 = beta*N/gamma`. (3 marks)

**Computed $R_0$ for the given parameters:**

$$R_0 = \frac{0.0005 \times 1000}{0.2} = \frac{0.5}{0.2} = 2.5$$

This satisfies $R_0 > 1$, so an epidemic will occur. (1 mark)

**Behaviour of $I(t)$:**
- When $R_0 < 1$: $I(t)$ rises briefly then decays exponentially to zero (no outbreak). (1 mark)
- When $R_0 > 1$: $I(t)$ rises to a peak, then decays to zero, giving the classic **epidemic curve**. (1 mark)
- For $R_0 = 2.5$: peak occurs at approximately $I_{\max} \approx 300$ infected at $t \approx 15$ days; the curve is asymmetric (faster rise, slower fall). (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing $R_0$ with $r$ (growth rate).** $R_0$ is dimensionless; $r$ has units of 1/time. (−1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the $1/N$ factor in mass-action kinetics.** The full form is $\beta SI/N$, not $\beta SI$. (−1 mark)
> 3. **Treating $R$ as stock that decreases** — it only ever increases monotonically in a closed-population SIR. (−1 mark)
> 4. **Writing the Stella equation as `S = -bSI/N`** instead of the correct differential form. Remember: the equation in the flow's box is the **rate**, not the level. (−2 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting to specify the integration method** (Euler vs RK4) when asked for implementation details. (−1 mark)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Stella = Vensim = Powersim** in mathematical content; they differ only in user-interface terminology.
- **Stock = level = state variable = integral of net flow.** It is what you *have*.
- **Flow = rate = derivative of a stock.** It is what *changes* the stock.
- **Converter = auxiliary = algebraic function** of stocks, flows, or other converters.
- **Connectors** carry **information**, not material.
- The four canonical biological models to memorise: **Malthusian, Logistic, Lotka-Volterra, SIR**.
- **Logistic equilibria:** $P^* = 0$ (unstable) and $P^* = K$ (stable).
- **Lotka-Volterra equilibria:** $X^* = \gamma/\delta$, $Y^* = \alpha/\beta$.
- **SIR threshold:** epidemic iff $R_0 = \beta N/\gamma > 1$.
- **Mass-action kinetics** always includes $1/N$ (or per-capita) for population-scale SIR.
- **Closed-population invariant:** $S + I + R = N$ (use this as a numerical-consistency check).
- **Solver choice:** RK4 with $\Delta t \le 0.01$ is sufficient for stiff-free biological systems; reduce to $\Delta t = 0.001$ if oscillation is observed.
- **Always** produce a **graphical output** (time-series + phase plot) — KTU practicals award 1–2 marks purely for the plot.
- **Software-neutrality principle:** If a viva asks "in which software?", answer with the **method** (stock-flow dynamics) and then name the tool — marks are awarded for the methodology, not the brand.
- **Negative feedback loop** → stability, equilibrium, homeostasis (e.g., immune vs pathogen).
- **Positive feedback loop** → exponential growth, runaway dynamics, tipping points.
- **Causal Loop Diagram (CLD)** uses signed arrows; **Stock-Flow Diagram (SFD)** uses rectangles + double-arrows. Both are required deliverables in the lab record.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Social System Simulation** is the application of *System Dynamics* (SD) methodology, developed by **Jay W. Forrester (MIT, 1961)**, to model and analyze the time-dependent behavior of socio-economic, demographic, epidemiological, urban, political, and behavioral systems. It uses **causal feedback structures**, **stocks (levels)**, **flows (rates)**, and **auxiliary variables (converters)** to mathematically represent the interdependencies, non-linearities, time-delays, and policy levers of human/social systems, and then numerically integrates these structures over time using dedicated graphical software environments such as **Vensim**, **Powersim**, or **Stella/iThink**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Checkpoint:** A social system in this experiment is treated as a *closed-loop feedback system* whose state evolves continuously with time. The model is *descriptive* (explains observed behavior) and *prescriptive* (tests policies virtually before real-world deployment).

### 1.2 Key Software Environments at a Glance

| Tool | Developer | Core Strength | File Extension |
|------|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Vensim** | Ventana Systems, USA | Powerful sensitivity & optimization, equation-based | `.mdl` |
| **Powersim** | Powersim Software AS, Norway | Industry-grade, real-time dashboards | `.sim` |
| **Stella / iThink** | isee systems, USA | Easiest GUI for beginners, educational focus | `.stmx` |

All three use the same underlying **System Dynamics grammar** (Forrester, 1961), differing only in interface and auxiliary features.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU lab manual accepts *any one* of the three tools. The exam, however, is **tool-agnostic** — questions test the underlying *concepts* (CLD, stock-flow, feedback polarity, stability), not the software brand.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bathtub" Model of Society

Imagine a city’s population as a **bathtub**:
- The **water level** inside the tub = the *stock* (e.g., number of people, infected individuals, literates).
- The **inlet tap** = *inflow rate* (births, immigration, new infections, new learners).
- The **outlet drain** = *outflow rate* (deaths, emigration, recovery, dropouts).
- The **hand turning the tap** = *converter/auxiliary* (policy decision, fertility rate, contact rate).
- The **feedback hose** from the tub back to the tap = the *causal feedback loop* (e.g., crowding reduces fertility, literacy drives more literacy).

A **simulation run** is simply watching the water rise or fall over simulated years as the taps are adjusted — except that in society, the taps themselves are dynamically controlled by the water level! This mutual causality is what makes social systems *non-linear* and *counter-intuitive*.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick for KTU viva:** Stocks = *state* (memory of the system). Flows = *action* (change per unit time). Converters = *knobs* (exogenous/policy levers). Connectors = *information links* (causal arrows).

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Building Blocks of a System Dynamics Model

1. **Stocks (Levels)** — Accumulations. Represent the *state* of the system at any instant. Discrete, cannot be negative.
2. **Flows (Rates)** — Activities causing stocks to change. Have units of *stock-unit / time*.
3. **Converters (Auxiliaries)** — Time-varying parameters or computed intermediate values (e.g., birth rate, GDP per capita).
4. **Connectors** — Causal arrows (information links) showing how one variable influences another without material flow.

### 2.2 Two Fundamental Differential Equation Forms

The dynamics of every stock, regardless of the social application, reduce to a **first-order ordinary differential equation (ODE)** of the form:

$$\frac{dS(t)}{dt} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Inflow}_i(t) \;-\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} \text{Outflow}_j(t)$$

where $S(t)$ is the stock value at simulated time $t$, and Inflows/Outflows may themselves depend on $S(t)$ (giving the *feedback* nature).

For numerical simulation, software tools internally apply the **Euler integration** (or higher-order **Runge–Kutta 4** for stiff systems) with a fixed time step $\Delta t$ (commonly $0.25$, $0.5$, or $1$ time-unit):

$$S(t+\Delta t) = S(t) + \left[ \sum \text{Inflow}(t) - \sum \text{Outflow}(t) \right] \cdot \Delta t$$

### 2.3 Feedback Loop Polarity — The Heart of Social Behavior

- **Reinforcing (Positive) Loop, R** : Behavior amplifies itself. *Runaway* growth or collapse. Example: *More literates → more teachers → more literates* (virtuous cycle) or *rumor spread* (vicious cycle).
- **Balancing (Negative) Loop, B** : Goal-seeking behavior. Counteracts deviation from a target. Example: *Higher population → lower per-capita food → lower fertility → population stabilizes* (carrying-capacity loop).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dominant-Loop Rule:** A system’s long-term behavior is governed by whichever feedback loop is **dominant at that time period**. Social systems almost always *switch dominance* (e.g., logistic growth: R-dominant first, then B-dominant near carrying capacity).

| 2 | Logistic (population) | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = r \cdot P \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{P}{K}\right)$ | persons / yr | **High** |

### 2.5 Engineering & Real-World Utility

- **Public Health:** Predicting epidemic peaks, optimizing vaccination rollout, lockdown policy testing (COVID-19, dengue, measles).
- **Urban Planning:** Simulating housing demand, traffic congestion, slum growth (Forrester’s *Urban Dynamics*, 1969).
- **Economics & Demographics:** National population models, labor-force forecasting, pension fund sustainability.
- **Corporate Strategy:** Market share evolution, technology substitution (Bass diffusion), product life-cycle.
- **Environmental Policy:** Deforestation, water-resource depletion, climate-policy impact assessment.
- **Defense & Sociology:** Counter-insurgency modeling, rumor/misinformation containment, riot spread.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Reference Model: Logistic Population Growth (Worked Example)

We will derive the *Verhulst (1838) logistic equation* from first principles as a system-dynamics stock-flow model — the single most-tested social system on KTU papers.

**Step 1 — Identify the stock.**
Let $P(t)$ = total population (the only stock). Units: *persons*.

**Step 2 — Identify the flows.**
- Birth inflow: $\text{Births} = b \cdot P(t)$, where $b$ = *crude birth rate* (persons/person/year).
- Death outflow: $\text{Deaths} = d \cdot P(t) + c \cdot P(t)^2 / K$, where $d$ = *natural death rate* and the quadratic term captures *crowding deaths* (disease, starvation) that rise with density, scaled by environmental *carrying capacity* $K$.

**Step 3 — Write the net rate equation (ODE).**

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \text{Births} - \text{Deaths} = bP - dP - \frac{c}{K}P^2$$

**Step 4 — Group the linear terms into intrinsic growth rate $r$.**

$$r = b - d \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{dt} = rP - \frac{c}{K}P^2$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $P$ to obtain the canonical logistic form.**

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = rP\left(1 - \frac{P}{K^*}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad K^* = \frac{rK}{c}$$

**Step 6 — Discrete Euler update for the simulator.**

$$P_{t+\Delta t} = P_t + r\,P_t\left(1 - \frac{P_t}{K^*}\right)\cdot\Delta t$$

**Step 7 — Fixed-point (steady-state) analysis.**

At equilibrium $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = 0$, giving two fixed points:

$$P^* = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad P^* = K^*$$

Stability check using $f'(P^*) = r\left(1 - 2P^*/K^*\right)$:
- At $P^* = 0$: $f'(0) = r > 0$ → **unstable** (any tiny seed population grows).
- At $P^* = K^*$: $f'(K^*) = -r < 0$ → **stable** (sigmoid saturation, the famous S-curve).

**Step 8 — Analytical solution for verification (separable ODE).**

$$\int \frac{dP}{P(1 - P/K^*)} = \int r\,dt \;\Rightarrow\; \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{K^*-P}\right) = rt + C_0 \;\Rightarrow\; P(t) = \frac{K^*}{1 + Ae^{-rt}}$$

where $A = \dfrac{K^*}{P_0} - 1$ is fixed by initial condition $P(0) = P_0$.

**Step 9 — Numerical sanity check** (let $r = 0.05$/yr, $K^* = 1000$ persons, $P_0 = 10$, $\Delta t = 1$ yr).

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 10 + 0.05 \cdot 10 \cdot (1 - 10/1000) \cdot 1 = 10 + 0.495 = 10.495 \\
P_2 &= 10.495 + 0.05 \cdot 10.495 \cdot 0.989505 \cdot 1 \approx 10.495 + 0.5191 = 11.014 \\
P_3 &\approx 11.014 + 0.05 \cdot 11.014 \cdot 0.988986 \approx 11.014 + 0.5447 = 11.559
\end{aligned}$$

The trajectory is rising but decelerating — exactly the expected early-phase S-curve.

### 3.2 Reference Model 2: SIR Epidemic (Board-Exam Favourite)

**Step 1 — Stocks.** $S(t)$ = Susceptibles, $I(t)$ = Infected, $R(t)$ = Recovered (all as fractions of total population $N = S+I+R$).

**Step 2 — Flows.**
- New infections per unit time: $\beta S I$ (mass-action contact, $\beta$ = transmission rate).
- Recoveries per unit time: $\gamma I$ ($\gamma$ = recovery rate = $1/\text{infectious period}$).

**Step 3 — ODE system.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dt} &= -\beta S I \\
\frac{dI}{dt} &= \beta S I - \gamma I \\
\frac{dR}{dt} &= \gamma I
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Threshold condition (epidemic occurs iff).**

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} \cdot S(0) > 1$$

**Step 5 — Peak infection time** (at $dI/dt = 0$, so $S^* = \gamma/\beta$):

$$t_{\text{peak}} = \frac{1}{\gamma(R_0-1)} \ln\!\left(\frac{S_0}{S^*}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Euler update for simulator (in Vensim/Powersim/Stella).**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{t+\Delta t} &= S_t - \beta \cdot S_t \cdot I_t \cdot \Delta t \\
I_{t+\Delta t} &= I_t + (\beta \cdot S_t \cdot I_t - \gamma \cdot I_t) \cdot \Delta t \\
R_{t+\Delta t} &= R_t + \gamma \cdot I_t \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in Vensim/Stella:** When the student types the `dt` expression, they are literally entering the right-hand side of the Euler integrator. The software then performs the loop automatically. Understanding step 6 demystifies the “black box” feel of GUI tools.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Common Social Models

| Model | Stocks | Flows | Key Feedback | KTU Typical Question |
|-------|--------|-------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Logistic population** | 1 (P) | Birth, Death | Balancing (carrying cap) | “Derive sigmoid curve” |
| **SIR epidemic** | 3 (S, I, R) | Infection, Recovery | Balancing (herd immunity) | “Predict peak time” |
| **Lotka–Volterra rumor** | 2 (Ignorant, Spreaders) | Contact, Forgetting | Reinforcing then balancing | “When does rumor die?” |
| **Bass diffusion (innovation)** | 2 (Adopters, Potential) | Innovation, Imitation | Reinforcing (network effect) | “Time to 50% adoption” |
| **World (Forrester 1971)** | 5+ (Pop, Capital, Resources, Pollution, Agri) | Multiple | Mixed (overshoot & collapse) | “Limits to Growth debate” |
| **Urban (Forrester 1969)** | 4 (Pop, Industry, Housing, Land) | Migration, Construction | Reinforcing then balancing | “Why do cities decay?” |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Causal Loop Diagram (CLD) — Generic Social System with Two Loops

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Population P] -- more crowding --> B[Fertility Rate b]
    A -- more infected contacts --> C[Disease Transmission β]
    B -- births --> A
    C -- deaths --> A
    A -- available teachers --> D[Literacy Rate L]
    D -- educated workforce --> E[Economic Output Y]
    E -- better healthcare --> C
    D -- better family planning awareness --> B
```

> Loop R1 (Population → Crowding → Fertility ↓ → Population) is **Balancing (B-loop)** — self-limiting.
> Loop R2 (Population → Literacy → Economy → Healthcare → Death rate ↓ → Population) is **Reinforcing (R-loop)** with a balancing converter — typical **S-shaped growth** behaviour.

### 4.2 Stock-Flow Diagram (SFD) — SIR Model as Built in Vensim/Stella

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Stock: Susceptible S] --|infection rate β·S·I|--> I[Stock: Infected I]
    I --|recovery rate γ·I|--> R[Stock: Recovered R]
    CloudIn(( )) -.->|births ν·N| S
    S -.->|deaths μ·S| CloudOut(( ))
    I -.->|deaths μ·I| CloudOut
    R -.->|deaths μ·R| CloudOut
```

The cloud symbols (`CloudIn`, `CloudOut`) represent **sources/sinks** outside the modeled boundary — a System Dynamics convention for un-modeled reservoirs.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define the terms “stock”, “flow” and “converter” in the context of system dynamics modelling of a social system. Give one example of each from a population model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Stock (Level):** A stock is an accumulation that represents the *state* of a system at a particular instant in time. It has memory — its current value depends on the entire history of its inflows and outflows. **Example:** Total population $P(t)$ of a city.
- **Flow (Rate):** A flow is an activity that *changes* a stock over time. It has the dimension of *stock-unit per unit time*. **Example:** Births per year feeding the population stock.
- **Converter (Auxiliary):** A converter is a time-varying or constant value that *converts* information flowing along connectors into numerical values for use in flow equations; it is itself not stocked. **Example:** Crude birth rate $b$ used inside the birth-flow equation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner trap:** Students frequently confuse *converters* with *stocks*. Remember: a converter is **never** an accumulation — it is a *computed* or *exogenous* intermediate. Markers specifically look for this distinction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a reinforcing feedback loop and a balancing feedback loop. Sketch the symbols used to denote each in a Causal Loop Diagram and give one social-system example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Reinforcing (R) Loop | Balancing (B) Loop |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Effect** | Amplifies change (runaway growth/collapse) | Opposes change (goal-seeking) |
| **Loop gain** | Product of link polarities = +1 | Product of link polarities = –1 |
| **Symbol** | `R` inside the loop arrow | `B` inside the loop arrow (sometimes `–` or `B`) |
| **Behaviour** | Exponential or hyperbolic (J/S curve) | Asymptotic approach to target (S-curve) |
| **Social example** | Literacy → teachers → literacy (virtuous cycle) | Higher population → crowding → reduced fertility → lower population |
| **Counter-example** | Rumor: more spreaders → more contacts → more spreaders (R) | Vaccination → fewer susceptible → herd immunity → epidemic ends (B) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 1-mark loss:** Writing “positive loop” instead of “reinforcing loop” — both are correct, but the standard KTU textbook term (Roberts, 1978) is *reinforcing*. Similarly, *balancing*, not *negative*.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Slot 1]**

*Model the spread of a contagious disease in a closed city of 50,000 people using the System Dynamics framework.*

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw the **Causal Loop Diagram (CLD)** and the **Stock-Flow Diagram (SFD)**. Identify the stocks, flows, converters and connectors. Clearly mark all feedback loops with R or B.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write the **set of differential equations** governing the system, the **Euler update equations** with $\Delta t = 1$ day, and compute the first **three time-steps** given $S(0) = 49990$, $I(0) = 10$, $R(0) = 0$, $\beta = 0.4$/day, $\gamma = 0.1$/day.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Diagram (3 marks for CLD + 2 marks for SFD + 2 marks for labelling):**

CLD (textual rendering — students must draw on paper):

```
S --contact--> I   (positive link, +)
I --recovery--> R  (positive link, +)
S ---> β  (info link)
I ---> γ  (info link)
R ---> herd immunity --> β effective  (negative link, –)
```

- **Reinforcing loop R1:** S → contact → I → contacts grow → S (vicious, the *epidemic* loop).
- **Balancing loop B1:** S → (low) S → I → R grows → herd-immunity → S protected (epidemic *ends*).

SFD (textual):
- **Stocks:** $S$, $I$, $R$ (three rectangles in Stella; three boxes in Vensim).
- **Flows:** *Infection rate* (valve between $S$ and $I$), *Recovery rate* (valve between $I$ and $R$).
- **Converters:** $\beta$ (transmission), $\gamma$ (recovery), with optional `Contact_Rate = β·S·I`.
- **Connectors:** $\beta$ → Infection rate; $I$ → Infection rate; $I$ → Recovery rate; $\gamma$ → Recovery rate.

**(b) Mathematical solution (7 marks: 2 for ODEs, 2 for Euler, 3 for numerical computation):**

ODEs:
$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta S I, \quad \frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \gamma I, \quad \frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$$

Euler updates with $\Delta t = 1$:
$$S_{t+1} = S_t - \beta S_t I_t, \quad I_{t+1} = I_t + (\beta S_t I_t - \gamma I_t), \quad R_{t+1} = R_t + \gamma I_t$$

**Step 1** (t=0 → 1):
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NewInf}_0 &= 0.4 \cdot 49990 \cdot 10 = 199{,}960 \\
S_1 &= 49990 - 199960 = -149970 \;\;(\text{physics broken — flag this!})
\end{aligned}$$

> This is the **classic KTU viva trap** — the student must recognize that a $\Delta t$ of 1 day is *far too coarse* for $\beta = 0.4$, which violates Euler stability. The proper answer is to **repeat with $\Delta t = 0.01$** (or 0.1).

**Step 1 (corrected, $\Delta t = 0.1$):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NewInf}_0 &= 0.4 \cdot 49990 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.1 = 19996 \\
S_1 &= 49990 - 19996 = 29994 \\
\text{NewRec}_0 &= 0.1 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.1 = 0.1 \\
I_1 &= 10 + 19996 - 0.1 = 20005.9 \\
R_1 &= 0 + 0.1 = 0.1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 (corrected):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NewInf}_1 &= 0.4 \cdot 29994 \cdot 20005.9 \cdot 0.1 \approx 0.4 \cdot 6.0006 \times 10^8 \cdot 0.1 = 24{,}000{,}240 \\
S_2 &\approx 29994 - 24000.24 = 5993.76 \\
I_2 &\approx 20005.9 + 24000.24 - (0.1\cdot 20005.9 \cdot 0.1) \approx 20005.9 + 24000.24 - 200.06 = 43806.08 \\
R_2 &\approx 0.1 + 200.06 = 200.16
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (corrected):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NewInf}_2 &= 0.4 \cdot 5993.76 \cdot 43806.08 \cdot 0.1 \approx 10{,}500{,}000 \text{ (unphysical)} \\
S_3 &\text{ would go negative again — confirms instability.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** With these parameters, $R_0 = \beta/\gamma = 4 > 1$, indicating a *fast outbreak* requiring very small $\Delta t \le 2/\beta = 5$ days. The student should report that *naive Euler* is **unstable** and recommend $\Delta t = 0.01$ day or use Vensim’s built-in RK4 integrator.

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 mark-losing pitfalls in Question A:**
> 1. Forgetting the **sum of stocks = constant** (in this closed model, $S + I + R$ must remain 50,000 ± rounding). Always verify closure.
> 2. Calling the *transmission rate* a stock — it is a **converter**. The marker will deduct.
> 3. Drawing the CLD without *loop labels* (R or B). You lose 2 marks flat.
