### 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Slot 2]**

*You are asked to model the adoption of a new mobile-payment app in a city of 1 million people using System Dynamics.*

**(a) [7 marks]** Build a **stock-flow model** with at least one reinforcing and one balancing loop. Clearly state your stocks, flows, converters and the equations of motion. Justify your choice of *carrying capacity* $K$ and *word-of-mouth coefficient* $p$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the **Bass diffusion model** in Euler form with $\Delta t = 1$ month, $p = 0.01$ (innovation), $q = 0.4$ (imitation), $K = 1{,}000{,}000$, and initial adopters $A(0) = 1000$, plot the **adoption curve** for the first 5 months and identify the **inflection point** (peak adoption rate) analytically.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Model Specification (7 marks):**

- **Stocks:** $A(t)$ = Adopters (accumulators of cumulative adoption), $P(t)$ = Potential non-adopters (so $A + P = K$).
- **Flows:** *NewAdopters(t)* (inflow to $A$, outflow from $P$).
- **Converters:** Innovation coefficient $p$, Imitation coefficient $q$, Word-of-mouth pressure $\alpha(t) = A(t)/K$.
- **Equations:**
$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \left(p + q\frac{A}{K}\right)(K - A)$$

- **CLD justification (1 mark):** Reinforcing loop R1: $A \uparrow$ → more imitation contact → $A \uparrow$ (viral growth). Balancing loop B1: $A \uparrow$ → $P = K-A$ ↓ → fewer potential adopters → $A$ growth slows (saturation). $K$ justified as the *total addressable market* (city population). $p$ justified as *mass-media effectiveness*; $q$ as *network/word-of-mouth strength* (large for social-tech products).

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

Euler form: $A_{t+1} = A_t + \big(p + q \cdot A_t/K\big)(K - A_t)\cdot\Delta t$.

Step 0: $A_0 = 1000$, $K = 1{,}000{,}000$, $p = 0.01$, $q = 0.4$, $\Delta t = 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= 1000 + \big(0.01 + 0.4\cdot 1000/10^6\big)(10^6 - 1000)\cdot 1 \\
    &= 1000 + (0.0104)(999{,}000) = 1000 + 10{,}389.6 = 11{,}389.6 \\
A_2 &= 11389.6 + (0.01 + 0.4\cdot 11389.6/10^6)(10^6 - 11389.6) \\
    &= 11389.6 + (0.014556)(988{,}610.4) = 11389.6 + 14{,}391.4 = 25{,}781.0 \\
A_3 &= 25781 + (0.01 + 0.4\cdot 25781/10^6)(10^6 - 25781) \\
    &= 25781 + (0.020312)(974{,}219) = 25781 + 19{,}790.4 = 45{,}571.4 \\
A_4 &= 45571.4 + (0.01 + 0.4\cdot 45571.4/10^6)(10^6 - 45571.4) \\
    &= 45571.4 + (0.028229)(954{,}428.6) = 45571.4 + 26{,}944.2 = 72{,}515.6 \\
A_5 &= 72515.6 + (0.01 + 0.4\cdot 72515.6/10^6)(10^6 - 72515.6) \\
    &= 72515.6 + (0.039006)(927{,}484.4) = 72515.6 + 36{,}180.0 = 108{,}695.6
\end{aligned}$$

**Inflection point (peak adoption rate):**
Setting $d^2A/dt^2 = 0$ yields the classical Bass result $A^* = K\!\left(1 - \sqrt{p/q}\right)/2$. Substituting:

$$A^* = 10^6 \cdot \frac{1 - \sqrt{0.01/0.4}}{2} = 10^6 \cdot \frac{1 - \sqrt{0.025}}{2} = 10^6 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.1581}{2} \approx 421{,}000 \text{ adopters}$$

This occurs approximately at simulated time $t^* = \dfrac{\ln(q/p)}{p+q} \approx \dfrac{\ln 40}{0.41} \approx \dfrac{3.689}{0.41} \approx 9$ months. The student should report this and note that **after month 5 we are still well in the R-loop-dominant phase** — adoption is *accelerating*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 mark-losing pitfalls in Question B:**
> 1. Treating $K$ as a *stock* in the SFD — it is a *constant converter*, not a level. The marker specifically looks for the **double-line** notation around constants in Stella.
> 2. Writing $A(t+1) = A(t) + p + q A/K$ — **forgetting the $(K-A)$ saturation factor** is the most common algebraic error.
> 3. Reporting the *peak adoption rate* $dA/dt$ without first locating the inflection point $A^*$ — partial credit is at most 2/7.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **GLOBAL PITFALLS — READ BEFORE YOUR EXAM**
> 1. **Tool brand bias:** Examiners do **not** award marks for naming Vensim/Powersim/Stella specifically. Marks are for *correct concepts* and *correct mathematics*. Memorize the theory, not the buttons.
> 2. **Arrow direction:** In a CLD, every arrow must have a *sign* (+/–) and a *polarity label* on the loop. Arrows without polarity = –1 mark per loop.
> 3. **Unit consistency:** A flow leaving stock $X$ (units: persons) must have units of *persons/time*. A common error is mixing *per-capita rates* with *absolute counts*.
> 4. **Validation triangle:** A model must be tested on three criteria — *structure validation* (does the CLD match expert knowledge?), *behaviour validation* (does the time-graph match historical data?), and *policy validation* (does it reproduce known policy outcomes?). Failing to mention validation = –2 marks.
> 5. **Boundary assumption:** Always state what is *inside* and *outside* the model boundary (e.g., births/deaths of a city may exclude migration if closed).
> 6. **Euler step sensitivity:** Always state your $\Delta t$. If the answer is unstable, reduce $\Delta t$ and explain the *Euler stability condition* $\Delta t \le 2/|\lambda_{\max}|$.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must be in your head before entering the exam hall):**
> - A **stock** = accumulation (state, has memory). A **flow** = activity per unit time. A **converter** = time-varying parameter or intermediate computation. A **connector** = information link (no material flow).
> - Every stock obeys $\frac{dS}{dt} = \sum\text{Inflow} - \sum\text{Outflow}$. Numerical simulation uses Euler: $S_{t+\Delta t} = S_t + (\text{In} - \text{Out})\Delta t$.
> - Two loop types: **Reinforcing (R)** = amplifies (positive feedback); **Balancing (B)** = goal-seeking (negative feedback). Label them inside loop arrows.
> - **Logistic growth**: $\frac{dP}{dt} = rP(1 - P/K^*)$ — one stock, one R-loop, one B-loop combined → S-curve.
> - **SIR epidemic**: three stocks $S, I, R$; threshold $R_0 = \beta/\gamma$; epidemic dies when $S$ falls below $\gamma/\beta$.
> - **Bass diffusion**: $\frac{dA}{dt} = (p + qA/K)(K - A)$ — innovation + imitation; inflection at $A^* = K(1 - \sqrt{p/q})/2$.
> - Vensim/Powersim/Stella are **tools**, not theory. The exam tests the *grammar* of system dynamics, not the GUI.
> - Always include a **causal loop diagram** *and* a **stock-flow diagram**; never one without the other.
> - Validate using **structure, behaviour, and policy** tests; report the validation outcome.
> - Check **Euler stability** by computing $\Delta t \le 2/|\lambda_{\max}|$; if violated, switch to RK4.
> - **Mass-action term** $\beta SI$ assumes homogeneous mixing — a known limitation of social SD models.
> - Common KTU social examples: **population dynamics, epidemic spread, technology adoption, rumor diffusion, urban decay, and resource depletion** — be ready to model any of these.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of industrial systems using Vensim/Powersim/Stella
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL607 - MODELLING AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of industrial systems using Vensim/Powersim/Stella

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Industrial System Simulation** is the process of building a computational, time-dependent replica of a real-world industrial process (such as a production line, inventory warehouse, supply chain, or manufacturing facility) using specialized **System Dynamics (SD)** software environments such as **Vensim**, **Powersim**, or **Stella/iThink**, in order to study, predict, and optimize the dynamic behaviour of the system over time without experimenting on the real plant.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for the Modelling and Simulation Lab (PCIEL607), this experiment is classified under the **Continuous System Simulation** category and is governed by the classical **Jay Forrester System Dynamics Methodology** — the same methodology that gave rise to these three iconic tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stock–Flow–Auxiliary (SFA) Paradigm**: Every industrial system simulated using Vensim, Powersim, or Stella is fundamentally represented using three primitive building blocks: **Stocks (Levels)**, **Flows (Rates)**, and **Auxiliary variables (Converters/Constants)**. This is the universal language shared by all three platforms.

### 1.2 The Three Software Platforms — At a Glance

| Tool | Origin | Primary Use in Lab |
|---|---|---|
| **Vensim** | Ventana Systems, USA | Academic-grade SD modeling, causal loop analysis |
| **Powersim** | Powersim Software AS, Norway | Enterprise-level industrial simulation, dashboards |
| **Stella/iThink** | isee systems, USA | Educational & conceptual SD modeling |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Intuition

Imagine an **industrial inventory warehouse** as a giant overhead water tank:

- The **water level inside the tank** = the **Stock (Inventory Level)** at any instant.
- The **inlet tap pouring water in** = the **Inflow Rate (Production / Replenishment Rate)**.
- The **outlet tap draining water out** = the **Outflow Rate (Demand / Shipment Rate)**.
- The **handle that opens/closes the taps** = the **Auxiliary variable (Decision Policy / Controller)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three taps and not one?** Because in a real factory, *production* and *consumption* happen *concurrently and continuously*. Vensim, Powersim, and Stella all solve the resulting differential equation $\frac{d(\text{Stock})}{dt} = \text{Inflow} - \text{Outflow}$ step-by-step using numerical integration (Euler or RK4) to give you the dynamic response curve over time.

### 1.4 Why Industrial Systems Need Simulation

Real industrial systems exhibit:
- **Non-linearity** (e.g., learning-curve effects, capacity saturation).
- **Time delays** (e.g., procurement lead time, transportation lag).
- **Feedback loops** (e.g., inventory adjusts production → production affects inventory).
- **Stochastic disturbances** (e.g., machine breakdowns, demand spikes).

These four properties make analytical (closed-form) solutions **impossible or grossly inaccurate**, which is exactly why we hand the system over to a numerical simulator like Vensim/Powersim/Stella.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Step System Dynamics Modelling Procedure

The universally accepted KTU-recommended procedure for simulating an industrial system is the **MIT Sloan five-step method** (Forrester, 1961), as adopted in the Vensim/Powersim/Stella manuals:

1. **Problem Articulation** — Define the industrial problem (e.g., "bullwhip effect in a 4-tier supply chain").
2. **Dynamic Hypothesis** — Sketch a **Causal Loop Diagram (CLD)** using positive (+) and negative (−) polarity links.
3. **Model Formulation** — Translate the CLD into a **Stock–Flow Diagram** with explicit mathematical equations.
4. **Simulation & Validation** — Run the model in Vensim/Powersim/Stella; compare output with historical plant data.
5. **Policy Analysis & Implementation** — Test alternative managerial policies; recommend the best one.

### 2.2 The Fundamental Stock–Flow Differential Equation

The bedrock equation of every industrial simulation built in Vensim/Powersim/Stella is:

$$
\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = R_{\text{in}}(t) - R_{\text{out}}(t)
$$

where:
- $L(t)$ = Stock (Level) at simulation time $t$ (units: items, kg, ₹, workers, etc.).
- $R_{\text{in}}(t)$ = Inflow rate (units / time).
- $R_{\text{out}}(t)$ = Outflow rate (units / time).

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrete vs. Continuous Form**: Vensim/Powersim/Stella internally convert the continuous ODE above into a *discrete-time difference equation* using a numerical integrator. The default integrator is **Euler's method** with time step $DT$:

$$
L(t + \Delta t) = L(t) + \big[\,R_{\text{in}}(t) - R_{\text{out}}(t)\,\big] \cdot \Delta t
$$

This is the equation silently executed millions of times by your laptop's CPU during a 5-year simulation run.

### 2.3 Higher-Order Behavioural Equations

Real industrial systems require non-linear laws. Common forms used in lab experiments:

**Linear (First-Order) System:**
$$
R_{\text{out}}(t) = \frac{L(t)}{\tau}
$$
where $\tau$ = average residence time (e.g., average time a part spends in the buffer).

**Saturation / Capacity-Limited Outflow:**
$$
R_{\text{out}}(t) = \min\!\left(\frac{L(t)}{\tau},\ C_{\max}\right)
$$
where $C_{\max}$ = maximum service rate of the workstation (e.g., 60 cars/hour).

**Goal-Seeking (Target Adjustment) Policy:**
$$
R_{\text{in}}(t) = \frac{L^{*} - L(t)}{T_{\text{adj}}}
$$
where $L^{*}$ = desired/desired inventory level and $T_{\text{adj}}$ = adjustment time constant.

**Information Delay (Material vs. Information Flow):**
$$
R_{\text{in}}(t) = \frac{1}{T_{\text{delay}}} \int_{t-T_{\text{delay}}}^{t} \text{Order}(s)\, ds
$$
modelled in software as an explicit **SMOOTH** or **DELAY** function.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L(t)$ | Stock (Level) | items, kg, ₹ | $\geq 0$ (physical constraint) |
| $R(t)$ | Flow (Rate) | items / time | Cannot be negative for outflows |
| $\Delta t$ | Integration time step | sec, min, day | Default in Vensim = 0.25 yr |
| $\tau$ | Time constant | time | $1/\tau$ = decay rate |
| $C_{\max}$ | Capacity ceiling | units / time | Hard cap on any flow |
| $T_{\text{delay}}$ | Information / transport delay | time | Use 1st, 3rd, or pipeline order |
| $L^{*}$ | Reference / goal level | same as $L$ | Set by decision-maker |
| $\text{SMOOTH}(x, T)$ | Exponential smoothing | — | Used in all three tools |
| $\text{DELAY}(x, T)$ | Material delay | — | Pipeline order 1–5 |
| $k$ | Gain constant | unitless | For amplifier-type policies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity-check rule for the lab exam**: Always verify $\sum(\text{Inflow}) - \sum(\text{Outflow}) = \frac{dL}{dt}$ for **every** stock in your model. If this conservation law is violated at any node, Vensim will throw a "Unit Mismatch" error and Powersim/Stella will display a red warning.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Toyota Production System** — Vensim was used to study the *andon* cord pull-rate dynamics.
- **Boeing Aircraft Assembly** — Stella/iThink models first- and second-tier supplier capacity.
- **Reliance Jamnagar Refinery** — Powersim Studio simulates crude oil inventory and distillation column throughput.
- **ISRO Launch Vehicle Integration** — Stella-based SD models of satellite launch cadence and workforce ramp-up.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — A Canonical Industrial System

**Worked Example:** A simple **Production–Inventory System** in which a factory produces at a constant rate $P = 50$ units/day and customers demand at a rate $D(t) = 10 + 0.5 \cdot t$ units/day, where $t$ is measured in days. Initial inventory $L(0) = 100$ units. We need to find the time $t^*$ at which the inventory first touches zero (the **stockout time**).

#### Step 1 — Define the Stock Equation
By definition of system dynamics:
$$
\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = P - D(t) = 50 - (10 + 0.5t)
$$

#### Step 2 — Simplify the Right-Hand Side
$$
\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = 40 - 0.5t
$$

#### Step 3 — Integrate Both Sides with Respect to $t$
$$
\int dL(t) = \int (40 - 0.5t)\, dt
$$
$$
L(t) = 40t - 0.25t^{2} + C
$$

#### Step 4 — Apply Initial Condition $L(0) = 100$
$$
L(0) = 40(0) - 0.25(0)^{2} + C = 100 \quad\Rightarrow\quad C = 100
$$

#### Step 5 — Final Closed-Form Solution
$$
L(t) = 100 + 40t - 0.25t^{2}
$$

#### Step 6 — Find the Stockout Time $t^*$
Set $L(t^*) = 0$:
$$
100 + 40t^{*} - 0.25(t^{*})^{2} = 0
$$
Multiply by $-4$ to standardise:
$$
(t^{*})^{2} - 160t^{*} - 400 = 0
$$
Apply the quadratic formula $t = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2} - 4ac}}{2a}$:
$$
t^{*} = \frac{160 \pm \sqrt{160^{2} - 4(1)(-400)}}{2(1)} = \frac{160 \pm \sqrt{25600 + 1600}}{2}
$$
$$
t^{*} = \frac{160 \pm \sqrt{27200}}{2} = \frac{160 \pm 164.92}{2}
$$
Taking the physically meaningful positive root:
$$
t^{*} = \frac{160 + 164.92}{2} = \frac{324.92}{2} = 162.46 \text{ days}
$$

#### Step 7 — Verification Using Numerical (Euler) Integration
This is the procedure Vensim/Powersim/Stella uses internally. With $\Delta t = 1$ day:

$$
L(t+1) = L(t) + (40 - 0.5t)\cdot \Delta t
$$

| Step $t$ (day) | $R_{\text{net}}(t) = 40 - 0.5t$ | $L(t)$ (units) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 40.0 | 100.0 |
| 10 | 35.0 | 470.0 |
| 50 | 15.0 | 1750.0 |
| 100 | −10.0 | 2750.0 |
| 150 | −35.0 | 1250.0 |
| 162 | −41.0 | 53.0 |
| 163 | −41.5 | 11.5 |
| **162.46** | — | **≈ 0 (stockout)** |

The numerical result $t^* \approx 162$ days matches the analytical result $t^* = 162.46$ days within $\Delta t = 1$ day, **validating** the model.

#### Step 8 — Implementation in Vensim/Powersim/Stella (Conceptual Sketch)

- **Stock `L`**: Inventory, initial value = 100.
- **Inflow `P`**: Constant = 50.
- **Outflow `D`**: Auxiliary = $10 + 0.5 \cdot \text{Time}$.
- **Net Flow**: $\text{Inflow} - \text{Outflow}$ connected to `L`.
- **Run Specs**: `INITIAL TIME = 0`, `FINAL TIME = 200`, `DT = 0.25`, `Integrator = Euler`.
- **Plot**: Graph of `L` vs `Time`; overlay a graph of the **derivative** $\frac{dL}{dt}$ to see the net rate cross zero.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Industrial Models

| Industrial Scenario | Stock | Inflow | Outflow | Key Auxiliary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Warehouse | Inventory | Replenishment | Sales rate | Reorder point |
| Workforce | Headcount | Hiring rate | Attrition rate | Desired workforce |
| Bank Account | Cash | Deposits | Withdrawals | Interest rate |
| Boiler | Steam pressure | Heater input | Turbine draw-off | Safety valve |
| Hospital Beds | Occupied beds | Admissions | Discharges | Target occupancy |

### 3.3 Numerical Integration Methods Offered by the Three Tools

| Method | Vensim | Powersim | Stella/iThink | Accuracy Order |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Euler (RK0) | ✔ Default | ✔ Default | ✔ Default | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t)$ |
| RK2 (Heun) | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t^{2})$ |
| RK4 | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t^{4})$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab tip**: For a stable, oscillation-free industrial simulation, use `DT ≤ 0.5 \times \text{(smallest time constant in the model)}`. If your fastest flow varies on a 1-day cycle, set `DT = 0.5` days or smaller.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; no reserved keywords used; labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric only (no bold/italic/HTML).

### 4.2 Diagram 1 — Universal Vensim/Powersim/Stella Simulation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START INDUSTRIAL SIMULATION]) --> stepB[DEFINE INDUSTRIAL PROBLEM AND BOUNDARIES]
    stepB --> stepC[IDENTIFY STOCKS LEVELS AND FLOWS RATES]
    stepC --> stepD[SKETCH CAUSAL LOOP DIAGRAM CLD]
    stepD --> stepE[BUILD STOCK FLOW DIAGRAM IN SOFTWARE]
    stepE --> stepF[ASSIGN MATHEMATICAL EQUATIONS TO EACH VARIABLE]
    stepF --> stepG[SET TIME BOUNDS AND INTEGRATION PARAMETERS]
    stepG --> stepH[RUN SIMULATION]
    stepH --> stepI{VALIDATE OUTPUT AGAINST HISTORICAL DATA}
    stepI -- MATCH --> stepJ[PERFORM POLICY SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS]
    stepI -- MISMATCH --> stepK[REFINE MODEL STRUCTURE AND PARAMETERS]
    stepK --> stepE
    stepJ --> stepL[GENERATE RECOMMENDATIONS FOR INDUSTRY]
    stepL --> endM([END REPORT GENERATION])
```

### 4.3 Diagram 2 — Generic Industrial Stock-Flow Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stockS[(INVENTORY STOCK L)] -- controlled by --> auxA[ORDER POLICY AUX]
    auxA -- demand signal --> inFlowR[PRODUCTION INFLOW R IN]
    inFlowR -- adds units per time --> stockS
    stockS -- consumed by --> outFlowR[CUSTOMER DEMAND OUTFLOW R OUT]
    outFlowR -- depletes stock --> stockS
    stockS -- measured by --> gaugeG[STOCKOUT DETECTOR]
    gaugeG -- feedback --> auxA
```

### 4.4 Diagram 3 — Software-Tool Comparison Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph coreEng[COMMON SIMULATION ENGINE]
        engineX[NUMERICAL ODE SOLVER]
        unitY[UNIT CONSISTENCY CHECKER]
        plotZ[TIME SERIES PLOTTER]
    end
    subgraph toolLayer[USER INTERFACE LAYER]
        vensimV[VENSIM PLE DSS]
        powersimP[POWERSIM STUDIO]
        stellaS[STELLA ITHINK]
    end
    vensimV --> engineX
    powersimP --> engineX
    stellaS --> engineX
    engineX --> unitY
    unitY --> plotZ
```

### 4.5 Diagram 4 — The Five-Step Forrester SD Methodology (Lab-Exam Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    f1[STEP 1 PROBLEM ARTICULATION] --> f2[STEP 2 DYNAMIC HYPOTHESIS CLD]
    f2 --> f3[STEP 3 MODEL FORMULATION STOCK FLOW]
    f3 --> f4[STEP 4 SIMULATION AND VALIDATION]
    f4 --> f5[STEP 5 POLICY ANALYSIS]
    f5 -- new policy --> f4
```

### 4.6 Diagram 5 — Closed-Loop Industrial Feedback Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    goalG[TARGET INVENTORY L STAR] --> compC[COMPARATOR DELTA L]
    stockL[(ACTUAL INVENTORY L)] --> compC
    compC --> policyP[ORDER POLICY RULE]
    policyP --> inFlowR[REPLENISHMENT RATE]
    inFlowR --> stockL
    stockL -- customer demand --> outFlowR[SHIPMENT RATE]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a "Stock" and a "Flow" as used in Vensim/Powersim/Stella. Give one industrial example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Stock** (also called Level) is an accumulation variable that represents the quantity of a resource present in an industrial system at a given instant of time. Mathematically it is the time integral of the net flow. *Example*: Number of finished goods in a factory warehouse.

A **Flow** (also called Rate) is a variable that represents the rate of change of a stock, measured in *units per unit time*. *Example*: Production rate of 50 cars/day entering the finished-goods warehouse.

Stocks are *state variables* (their value at any time depends on history); flows are *rate variables* (they can be changed instantaneously by the decision-maker). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is a Causal Loop Diagram (CLD)? Why is it drawn before the stock-flow diagram in industrial system simulation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Causal Loop Diagram (CLD)** is a qualitative map of an industrial system that shows how key variables influence one another through causal links marked with **positive (+)** or **negative (−)** polarity. These links form **reinforcing (R)** or **balancing (B)** feedback loops.

It is drawn *before* the stock-flow diagram because: (i) it forces the modeller to identify the dominant feedback structure of the system, (ii) it helps in detecting unintended consequences (e.g., bullwhip effect), and (iii) it serves as a communication bridge between domain experts and simulation engineers before committing to precise equations. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*(a)* Explain the five-step System Dynamics methodology proposed by Jay Forrester for simulating an industrial system. Use a block diagram to illustrate. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A manufacturing unit has an inventory level that decays exponentially. The stock $L(t)$ satisfies $\frac{dL}{dt} = -kL$ with $L(0) = L_0$. Derive the closed-form solution and find the time at which the stock falls to 1% of its initial value, given $k = 0.05$/day. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Step 1 – Problem Articulation (1 mark):** Define the industrial problem, scope, time horizon, and key performance indicators (KPIs) such as throughput, WIP, lead time.
2. **Step 2 – Dynamic Hypothesis (1 mark):** Draw a Causal Loop Diagram identifying reinforcing and balancing loops; for example, the *Workforce–Backlog* loop in a job shop.
3. **Step 3 – Model Formulation (2 marks):** Convert the CLD into a Stock–Flow Diagram in Vensim/Powersim/Stella. Define state equations of the form $\frac{dL}{dt} = R_{\text{in}} - R_{\text{out}}$ for every stock, and algebraic equations for every auxiliary.
4. **Step 4 – Simulation & Validation (2 marks):** Set `INITIAL TIME`, `FINAL TIME`, `DT`, and integration method; run the model; statistically compare simulated output with historical plant data (e.g., MAPE < 10% is the KTU benchmark).
5. **Step 5 – Policy Analysis (1 mark):** Test alternative managerial policies (e.g., level-vs-rate production) and recommend the best using sensitivity graphs.

A representative block flow is:

```
PROBLEM -> CLD -> STOCK FLOW -> EQUATIONS -> RUN -> VALIDATE -> POLICY TEST
```

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

The governing equation is the first-order linear ODE:
$$
\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = -kL(t), \quad L(0) = L_0
$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables (1 mark):**
$$
\frac{dL}{L} = -k\, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides (1 mark):**
$$
\int_{L_0}^{L(t)} \frac{dL}{L} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$
$$
\ln L(t) - \ln L_0 = -kt
$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate (1 mark):**
$$
L(t) = L_0\, e^{-kt}
$$

**Step 4 — Find the time at which $L(t_{1\%}) = 0.01 L_0$ (2 marks):**
$$
0.01 L_0 = L_0\, e^{-k t_{1\%}}
$$
$$
0.01 = e^{-k t_{1\%}}
$$
Take natural logarithm of both sides:
$$
\ln(0.01) = -k t_{1\%}
$$
$$
t_{1\%} = \frac{-\ln(0.01)}{k} = \frac{4.6052}{0.05} = 92.10 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5 — Vensim/Powersim/Stella verification (2 marks):**
The closed-form $L(t) = L_0 e^{-0.05 t}$ is implemented as an auxiliary *Exponential Decay* function. Plot of `L` vs `Time` shows the same 92.10-day crossing point, validating the analytical derivation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**
*(a)* Compare and contrast **Vensim**, **Powersim**, and **Stella/iThink** as industrial simulation tools. Mention the equation syntax of each. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A two-stage production line has stocks $L_1$ and $L_2$. The flows are $R_{12} = 5$ units/hr from $L_1$ to $L_2$ and $R_{2\text{out}} = \frac{L_2}{4}$ units/hr. Given $L_1(0) = 200$, $L_2(0) = 50$, and the inflow to $L_1$ is $R_{\text{in}} = 10$ units/hr, derive the steady-state values of $L_1$ and $L_2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Feature | Vensim | Powersim | Stella/iThink |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vendor | Ventana Systems | Powersim AS | isee systems |
| Equation syntax | `L = INTEG(Rin - Rout, L0)` | `L[t] = L[t-1] + DT*(Rin-Rout)` | `L(t) = L(t-dt) + (Rin-Rout)*dt` |
| Graphing | SyntheSim, sensitivity | Step charts, dashboards | Map, sector diagrams |
| Suitability | Academic, policy | Enterprise, ERP | Education, conceptual |
| Free version | Vensim PLE | Powersim Express | Stella Online (trial) |
| Causal loop tool | Built-in | Plugin | Built-in |
| Pricing model | Academic discount | Commercial | Education bundle |

(Allocate ~1 mark per meaningful row; tabulated = 7 marks)

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Stock 1 equation (1 mark):**
$$
\frac{dL_1}{dt} = R_{\text{in}} - R_{12} = 10 - 5 = 5 \text{ units/hr (constant)}
$$

**Stock 2 equation (1 mark):**
$$
\frac{dL_2}{dt} = R_{12} - R_{2\text{out}} = 5 - \frac{L_2}{4}
$$

**Steady-state condition for $L_1$ (1 mark):**
At steady state, $\frac{dL_1}{dt} = 0$:
$$
0 = 5 \Rightarrow \text{Impossible!}
$$
Therefore **Stock 1 grows without bound** linearly: $L_1(t) = 200 + 5t$, meaning the system is **not at steady state for $L_1$** — it is a buffer that keeps filling.

**Steady-state condition for $L_2$ (2 marks):**
At steady state, $\frac{dL_2}{dt} = 0$:
$$
0 = 5 - \frac{L_2^{*}}{4} \Rightarrow L_2^{*} = 20 \text{ units}
$$

**Time to reach steady state (1 mark):** Solve $\frac{dL_2}{dt} + \frac{L_2}{4} = 5$. The solution is:
$$
L_2(t) = L_2^{*} + (L_2(0) - L_2^{*}) e^{-t/4} = 20 + 30 e^{-t/4}
$$
At $t = 4\tau = 16$ hours, $L_2 \approx 20 + 30(0.018) \approx 20.55$ units (98% of steady state).

**Vensim/Powersim/Stella implementation (1 mark):** Two stocks, one constant, one first-order outflow, time constants = 4 hr, run for 50 hours.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes observed in KTU valuation scripts for this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the equation sheet — Examiner deducts **1 mark** per stock with no units.
> 2. **Confusing Causal Loop Diagrams (CLDs)** with Stock–Flow Diagrams (SFDs). CLDs use words and arrows with +/−; SFDs use **boxes (stocks)**, **double-lined arrows (flows)**, and **valves (rate controls)**.
> 3. **Writing $L(t+1) = L(t) + \Delta t \cdot R(t+1)$** instead of the correct Euler forward form $L(t+1) = L(t) + \Delta t \cdot R(t)$. The future-rate variant is *implicit* Euler and is **not** the default in Vensim.
> 4. **Not setting `INITIAL TIME = 0`** — the model will silently use the software's default (often the year of installation) and your plots will look bizarre.
> 5. **Mixing time units** (e.g., demand per *day* but production per *hour*). This causes a *unit-mismatch error* that the software will flag, but many students ignore it and lose **2 marks** in the validation step.
> 6. **Drawing a CLD without polarity signs** — both + and − are mandatory, or the loop type (R/B) cannot be determined.
> 7. **Ignoring the "no negative stocks" physical constraint** in industrial models (you cannot have −5 cars in a warehouse).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Industrial system simulation = numerical ODE solving in Vensim/Powersim/Stella using the **Stock–Flow** paradigm.
- [x] Three primitive elements: **Stock (Level)**, **Flow (Rate)**, **Auxiliary (Converter)**.
- [x] Fundamental ODE: $\frac{dL}{dt} = R_{\text{in}} - R_{\text{out}}$; discrete Euler form: $L_{t+\Delta t} = L_t + (R_{\text{in}} - R_{\text{out}})\Delta t$.
- [x] Two diagram types: **CLD** (qualitative, +/− polarity, R/B loops) and **SFD** (quantitative, with boxes and valves).
- [x] Five-step Forrester methodology: **Problem → CLD → SFD → Simulate/Validate → Policy Analysis**.
- [x] Vensim default equation: `L = INTEG(Rin - Rout, L0)`.
- [x] Powersim default equation: `L[t] = L[t-1] + DT*(Rin - Rout)`.
- [x] Stella default equation: `L(t) = L(t-dt) + (Rin - Rout)*dt`.
- [x] Three canonical delays: **SMOOTH** (information), **DELAY1** (first-order material), **PIPELINE** (multi-stage).
- [x] Three canonical nonlinearities: **Saturation** (min function), **Goal-seeking** (target-adjustment), **S-curve growth** (logistic).
- [x] **Stability rule**: `DT ≤ 0.5 × (smallest time constant in the model)`.
- [x] **Conservation check**: $\sum R_{\text{in}} - \sum R_{\text{out}} = \frac{dL}{dt}$ for every stock — verify units!
- [x] **Validation benchmark**: MAPE < 10% versus historical plant data.
- [x] **Lab viva favourite**: "If you double the demand, what happens to inventory?" — answer using the closed-form $L(t)$ and the sensitivity graph.
- [x] **Software of choice for KTU labs**: **Vensim PLE** (free) or **Stella Online** (trial) — both are accepted in the exam record.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Apply system dynamics models for forecasting, financial analysis and decision making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL607 - MODELLING AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Apply system dynamics models for forecasting, financial analysis and decision making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Dynamics (SD)** is a methodology and mathematical-modeling technique developed by **Professor Jay W. Forrester** at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) in **1956**. It is formally defined as the *computer-aided study of the behavior of complex socio-technical systems over time using the principles of feedback control theory, information flow, and non-linear dynamics*.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCIEL607 – Modelling and Simulation Lab)**, System Dynamics is treated as a **continuous-time, deterministic simulation paradigm** that allows engineers to:

1. Build *causal* (cause-and-effect) representations of a real-world system.
2. Translate those representations into **differential equations** using *Stocks* and *Flows*.
3. Simulate the model on software platforms such as **Vensim PLE, STELLA/iThink, Powersim, or MATLAB/Simulink** to obtain long-term forecasts and to test *what-if* policy decisions.

### 1.2 The Three Canonical Application Domains

| Application Domain | Purpose of SD Model | Typical Stock | Typical Flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forecasting** | Predict long-term market or population behavior | Customers, Population | Adoption rate, Birth rate |
| **Financial Analysis** | Project cash flow, ROI, payback, NPV | Cash, Debt, Equity | Revenue, Expenses, Interest |
| **Decision Making** | Test policy levers before implementation | Inventory, Workforce | Hiring, Production, Sales |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Bathtub Model"

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bathtub Analogy** is the canonical intuition-building metaphor used in every System Dynamics textbook. Imagine a bathtub:
> - The **water level** inside the tub is the **Stock** (state variable).
> - The **faucet** is the **Inflow** (rate that *adds* to the stock).
> - The **drain** is the **Outflow** (rate that *removes* from the stock).
> - The **person watching the tub** and adjusting the faucet based on how full the tub is represents a **Feedback Loop** (closing the cause-effect circle).
> - The **time it takes for hot water to reach the shower head** is a **Delay**.

If the inflow is greater than the outflow, the stock *rises*; if outflow is greater, the stock *falls*. This single mental image captures the entire philosophy of System Dynamics.

### 1.4 Why System Dynamics for Engineering Problems?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard engineering metrics** used in SD modeling:
> - **Time constant (τ)** – measured in seconds, days, or years depending on the system. 
> - **Loop dominance** – dimensionless indicator of which feedback loop is driving behavior.
> - **Sensitivity index** – dimensionless ratio of %change in output to %change in input parameter.
> - **Step response** – output magnitude measured against an idealized unit-step input.

Classical engineering tools (calculus, control theory, statistics) often fail when a system contains **non-linear feedback** and **time delays** because they require closed-form solutions. System Dynamics embraces these complexities and solves them numerically using **Euler integration** or **Runge-Kutta 4th order (RK4)** methods embedded in the simulation engine.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Five Primitive Building Blocks

Every System Dynamics model, regardless of complexity, is composed of exactly **five primitive elements**. KTU examiners frequently test these directly.

1. **Stocks (Levels / State Variables)**
   - Represent *accumulations* – things you can *measure at an instant*.
   - Units: persons, rupees, units, kilograms.
   - Notation in STELLA/Vensim: a rectangle.
   - They have *memory* – their current value depends on the entire history of inflows and outflows.

2. **Flows (Rates)**
   - Represent *activities* that *change* stocks over time.
   - Units: persons/month, ₹/year, kg/day.
   - Notation: a thick arrow with a "tap" (valve) symbol.
   - They are the *only* elements allowed to change stocks.

3. **Auxiliary Variables (Converters)**
   - Intermediate calculations that do not accumulate.
   - Example: *Profit = Revenue – Cost*; *Interest = Principal × Rate*.

4. **Connectors (Information Links)**
   - Thin arrows that show the *flow of information* (not material) from one element to another.

5. **Constants**
   - Fixed parameters (e.g., growth rate, conversion factor, initial value).

### 2.2 Feedback Loop Theory – The Heart of System Dynamics

> [!NOTE]
> A **Feedback Loop** is a closed chain of causal connections where the *output* of a process eventually influences its own *input*. There are exactly two polarities:

#### 2.2.1 Reinforcing Loop (R-loop) – "Virtuous or Vicious Cycle"

- An increase in a variable propagates through the loop and produces a **further increase** in the same variable.
- Generates **exponential growth** or **exponential collapse**.
- Examples: compound interest, viral spread, debt spirals, network effects.
- Behavior: $S(t) = S_0 e^{\lambda t}$, where $\lambda$ is the *loop gain*.

#### 2.2.2 Balancing Loop (B-loop) – "Goal-Seeking Behavior"

- An increase in a variable eventually causes a *decrease* in that variable.
- Generates **S-shaped (logistic) growth** or **oscillatory damping**.
- Examples: a thermostat, predator-prey systems, market saturation.
- Behavior: $S(t) = \dfrac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K - S_0}{S_0}\right) e^{-rt}}$ (logistic solution).

### 2.3 The Stock-Flow Differential Equation (Core Equation)

The single most important equation in the entire System Dynamics syllabus is:

$$\frac{dS(t)}{dt} = \text{Inflow}(t) - \text{Outflow}(t)$$

This is integrated numerically by the simulation engine using the **Euler discretization**:

$$S(t + \Delta t) = S(t) + \Delta t \cdot \big[\text{Inflow}(t) - \text{Outflow}(t)\big]$$

The KTU-recommended time step is **$\Delta t$ = DT** (default 0.25 time units), and the recommended integration method is **RK4 (4th-order Runge-Kutta)** for non-stiff systems.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorise this table verbatim. Every value below is a *high-frequency* KTU question.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variable Definition | Units |
|:-:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stock Update (Euler)** | $S_{t+\Delta t} = S_t + \Delta t \cdot (I_t - O_t)$ | $I$=Inflow, $O$=Outflow | Stock units |
| 2 | **Exponential Growth (R-loop)** | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = rP$ | $r$ = net growth rate | 1/time |
| 3 | **Logistic Growth (B-loop with capacity)** | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = rP\!\left(1 - \dfrac{P}{K}\right)$ | $K$ = carrying capacity | persons |
| 4 | **Compound Interest (Finance)** | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = r \cdot V(t)$ | $V$=capital, $r$=rate | ₹ |
| 5 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $\text{CF}_t$ = cash flow at $t$ | ₹ |
| 6 | **Bass Diffusion (Market Forecast)** | $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = \!\left(p + q \dfrac{N}{m}\right)\!(m - N)$ | $p$ = innovation, $q$ = imitation, $m$ = market potential | adopters |
| 7 | **First-Order Exponential Delay** | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = \dfrac{\text{Desired} - S}{\tau}$ | $\tau$ = time constant | time |
| 8 | **Inventory Balance (Forrester)** | $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \text{Production} - \text{Sales}$ | $I$ = on-hand stock | units |
| 9 | **Workforce Dynamics** | $\dfrac{dW}{dt} = \text{Hires} - \text{Attrition}$ | $W$ = headcount | persons |
| 10 | **Exponential Smoothing (Decision Support)** | $\hat{y}_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{y}_t$ | $\alpha$ = smoothing constant $\in [0,1]$ | dimensionless |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Engineering** – bullwhip effect, production-inventory control.
- **Civil Engineering** – urban growth forecasting, traffic congestion feedback.
- **Biomedical Engineering** – glucose-insulin regulatory system, epidemiology.
- **Financial Engineering** – capital flow, credit-default cascades.
- **Environmental Engineering** – reservoir management, climate-feedback systems.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often award **partial credit** if a student can correctly identify the *stocks* and *flows* of a verbal problem even if the simulation step is incomplete. Always begin your answer by explicitly listing **"Stocks = …, Flows = …, Auxiliary = …"** before writing equations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Generic Stock-Flow Discretization

We begin with the continuous-time stock equation:

$$\frac{dS(t)}{dt} = I(t) - O(t)$$

Multiply both sides by $dt$:

$$dS(t) = \big[I(t) - O(t)\big]\,dt$$

Integrate from $t$ to $t + \Delta t$:

$$\int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} dS = \int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} \big[I(\tau) - O(\tau)\big]\,d\tau$$

Assuming $I$ and $O$ are constant over $[t, t+\Delta t]$ (the Euler assumption), the right-hand integral simplifies to:

$$S(t+\Delta t) - S(t) = \big[I(t) - O(t)\big]\Delta t$$

Rearranging:

$$S(t+\Delta t) = S(t) + \Delta t \cdot \big[I(t) - O(t)\big]$$

This is the **fundamental update rule** used by every SD engine (Vensim, STELLA, Simulink) at every simulation step. The KTU-expected *default* is **DT = 0.25** for continuous-time systems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – Market Growth Forecasting (Exponential Model)

Consider a *closed* market segment with no entry/exit, growing at a net compounding rate $r$ per year.

**Step 1 – Identify primitives:**
- Stock: Customers $N(t)$
- Flow: $\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ where net flow $= r \cdot N(t)$

**Step 2 – Write the ODE:**

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = r \cdot N(t)$$

**Step 3 – Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dN}{N} = r\,dt$$

**Step 4 – Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:**

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = \int_{0}^{t} r\,dt$$

**Step 5 – Evaluate:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N(t)}{N_0}\right) = rt$$

**Step 6 – Exponentiate:**

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{rt}$$

This is the **Exponential Growth** (pure R-loop) solution.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU 3-mark style):**
- $N_0 = 1000$ customers, $r = 0.08$/year.
- Find $N(5)$.

$$N(5) = 1000 \cdot e^{0.08 \times 5} = 1000 \cdot e^{0.4}$$

$$e^{0.4} \approx 1.4918 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N(5) \approx 1491.8 \text{ customers}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – Logistic Growth (R + B Combined)

A pure R-loop is unrealistic because every market saturates. The **Logistic Equation** adds a balancing feedback via a *carrying capacity* $K$ (total addressable market).

**Step 1 – Modified flow:**

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = r \cdot N(t) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{N(t)}{K}\right)$$

**Step 2 – Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dN}{N\!\left(1 - \dfrac{N}{K}\right)} = r\,dt$$

**Step 3 – Use partial fractions** (since $\dfrac{1}{N(1 - N/K)} = \dfrac{1}{N} + \dfrac{1/K}{1 - N/K}$):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \!\!\left(\frac{1}{N} + \frac{1}{K - N}\right) dN = \int_0^{t} r\,dt$$

**Step 4 – Integrate:**

$$\Big[\ln N - \ln(K - N)\Big]_{N_0}^{N(t)} = rt$$

**Step 5 – Simplify:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N(t)}{K - N(t)}\right) - \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{K - N_0}\right) = rt$$

**Step 6 – Solve for $N(t)$:**

$$N(t) = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K - N_0}{N_0}\right) e^{-rt}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **S-shaped (sigmoid) curve** is the *fingerprint* of any system with one R-loop and one B-loop competing. Examples: technology adoption, disease spread with recovery, inventory build-up.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 – Financial Cash-Flow Model (Decision Making)

A startup has monthly Revenue $R(t)$ and Expenses $E(t)$. The stock of interest is **Cash Balance** $C(t)$.

**Step 1 – Primitives:**
- Stock: $C(t)$
- Inflow: Revenue $R(t) = p \cdot s(t)$, where $p$ = price, $s(t)$ = units sold
- Outflow: Expenses $E(t) = \text{fixed} + \text{variable cost}$
- Auxiliary: Profit $\Pi(t) = R(t) - E(t)$

**Step 2 – Stock equation:**

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = R(t) - E(t)$$

**Step 3 – Euler update:**

$$C_{t+\Delta t} = C_t + \Delta t \cdot \big[R_t - E_t\big]$$

**Step 4 – Net Present Value (NPV) over $T$ years:**

The NPV of the project, with discount rate $r$, is:

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{\Pi(t)}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$$

If $\text{NPV} > 0$, the project is *financially viable* (decision rule).

**Step 5 – Internal Rate of Return (IRR):**
IRR is the value $r^*$ that makes NPV $= 0$:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{\Pi(t)}{(1+r^*)^t} - C_0 = 0$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 – Bass Diffusion Model (Product Forecasting)

The **Bass Diffusion Model** (1969) is the gold-standard for forecasting new-product adoption.

**Step 1 – Cumulative adopters** is the stock $N(t)$; total potential market is $m$.

**Step 2 – Adoption rate is driven by two forces:**
- *Innovation* (independent adoption): rate $p \cdot [m - N(t)]$
- *Imitation* (word-of-mouth): rate $q \cdot \dfrac{N(t)}{m} \cdot [m - N(t)]$

**Step 3 – Combine:**

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \left[p + q \cdot \frac{N(t)}{m}\right] \big[m - N(t)\big]$$

- $p$ = coefficient of innovation $\approx 0.001$ to $0.03$ (typical for durable goods)
- $q$ = coefficient of imitation $\approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ (typical for fads/fashion)

**Step 4 – Closed-form solution (Bemmaor reference):**

$$N(t) = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"Sketch the Bass curve and identify the inflection point."* The inflection point occurs at $N^* = m \cdot \dfrac{p}{q} \left(\sqrt{\dfrac{q}{p}} - 1\right)$ — memorise this fact.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix – Mapping SD Primitives to the Three Application Domains

| Application | Stock | Inflow | Outflow | Key Feedback Loop | Decision Lever |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sales Forecasting** | Customers $N$ | Adoption | Churn | R-loop (word-of-mouth) + B-loop (saturation) | Marketing spend |
| **Cash Management** | Cash $C$ | Revenue | Expenses | B-loop (working capital) | Credit policy |
| **Inventory** | On-hand $I$ | Production | Sales | B-loop (reorder point) | Order quantity |
| **Workforce Planning** | Headcount $W$ | Hires | Attrition | R + B | Training budget |
| **Epidemic** | Infected $I$ | New infections | Recoveries | R-loop | Vaccination rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **full marks** in any KTU 14-mark question, students must:
> 1. **List primitives** (Stock, Flow, Aux, Constant).
> 2. **Draw the Causal Loop Diagram (CLD)** with R/B loop labels.
> 3. **Write the ODE** with explicit $\dfrac{dS}{dt}$ form.
> 4. **Solve** analytically *and* state the Euler update rule.
> 5. **Interpret** the result (e.g., "Growth is exponential until $N \approx 0.5K$, then saturates").
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 The System Dynamics Modelling Process (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Problem Articulation] --> B[Step 2: System Boundary & Time Horizon]
    B --> C[Step 3: Identify Stocks, Flows, Auxiliaries]
    C --> D[Step 4: Draw Causal Loop Diagram CLD]
    D --> E[Step 5: Build Stock and Flow Diagram]
    E --> F[Step 6: Write Differential Equations]
    F --> G[Step 7: Parameter Estimation from Data]
    G --> H[Step 8: Simulate in Vensim STELLA or Simulink]
    H --> I{Step 9: Validation}
    I -->|Match Real Data| J[Step 10: Policy Analysis and Decision]
    I -->|Mismatch| C
    J --> K[Forecasting and Reporting]
```

### 4.2 Causal Loop Diagram – Market Growth with Customer Satisfaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Customers((Customers)) -->|more| WordOfMouth
    WordOfMouth[Word of Mouth] -->|increases| NewAdoptions
    NewAdoptions[New Adoptions] -->|adds to| Customers
    Customers -->|saturates| MarketPotential
    MarketPotential[Market Potential] -->|reduces| NewAdoptions
    Customers -->|feeds| ServiceQuality
    ServiceQuality[Service Quality] -->|enhances| WordOfMouth
```

> The first three elements form a **Reinforcing Loop (R)**. The `Customers → MarketPotential → NewAdoptions → Customers` chain is a **Balancing Loop (B)**. The two loops compete, producing classic S-curve (logistic) adoption.

### 4.3 Stock and Flow Diagram – Generic SD Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Aux1[Auxiliary Variable] -->|info| FlowIn
    FlowIn([Inflow Rate]) -->|material| Stock
    Stock[[Stock Level]] -->|material| FlowOut
    FlowOut([Outflow Rate]) -->|material| Drain((Sink))
    Aux2[Other Auxiliary] -->|info| FlowOut
    Constant[Constant Parameter] -->|value| Aux1
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture – Software Tool Mapping for PCIEL607 Lab

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT
        P1[Verbal Problem Statement]
        P2[Historical Data Time Series]
        P3[Domain Expert Knowledge]
    end
    subgraph MODEL_BUILD
        M1[Causal Loop Diagram CLD]
        M2[Stock Flow Diagram SFD]
        M3[Mathematical Equations ODEs]
    end
    subgraph SIMULATION_ENGINE
        S1[Vensim PLE]
        S2[STELLA Architect]
        S3[MATLAB Simulink]
        S4[Powersim Studio]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[Time Series Plots]
        O2[Policy Sensitivity Table]
        O3[Decision Recommendation]
    end
    P1 --> M1
    P2 --> M3
    P3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2 --> M3
    M3 --> S1
    M3 --> S2
    M3 --> S3
    M3 --> S4
    S1 --> O1
    S1 --> O2
    S2 --> O1
    S3 --> O1
    S3 --> O2
    S1 --> O3
    S2 --> O3
```

### 4.5 Reference Example – CLD for Financial Cash-Flow Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CashBalance((Cash Balance)) -->|funds| MarketingSpend
    MarketingSpend[Marketing Spend] -->|drives| NewSales
    NewSales[New Sales] -->|generates| Revenue
    Revenue[Revenue] -->|inflow to| CashBalance
    CashBalance -->|pays| OperatingExpenses
    OperatingExpenses[Operating Expenses] -->|reduces| CashBalance
    CashBalance -->|low balance triggers| CostCutting
    CostCutting[Cost Cutting] -->|reduces| OperatingExpenses
```

> Loop A: `Cash → Marketing → Sales → Revenue → Cash` is a **Reinforcing Loop** (R).
> Loop B: `Cash → Expenses → (depletes) Cash` is a **Balancing Loop** (B).
> Loop C: `Cash → Cost Cutting → Expenses` is a corrective **Balancing Loop**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mermaid Pitfall:** Never name a node with a reserved keyword such as `end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, or `style` as a standalone ID. Use alphanumeric prefixed names (e.g., `loopA`, `stock1`).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define System Dynamics. List the five primitive building blocks used in any System Dynamics model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** System Dynamics is a computer-aided methodology developed by Jay W. Forrester (MIT, 1956) for studying the time-dependent behaviour of complex socio-technical systems through the interaction of stocks, flows, and feedback loops.
- **Five primitives (1.5 marks):** (i) Stocks (Levels), (ii) Flows (Rates), (iii) Auxiliary Variables (Converters), (iv) Connectors (Information Links), (v) Constants.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Differentiate between a Reinforcing loop (R) and a Balancing loop (B). Give one engineering example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Reinforcing Loop (1.5 marks):** An R-loop amplifies the deviation from equilibrium, producing *exponential growth* or *exponential collapse*. Example: compound interest on a savings account, viral spread of a technology.
- **Balancing Loop (1.5 marks):** A B-loop counteracts deviation, producing *goal-seeking* or *S-shaped* behaviour. Example: a thermostat controlling room temperature, market adoption approaching saturation.

---

### 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A – 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **methodology of System Dynamics modelling**. List the major steps in order. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Develop a **System Dynamics model for forecasting product adoption in a new market** using the Bass Diffusion framework. Define every variable, write the governing differential equation, derive the closed-form solution, and find the inflection point. *(7 marks)*

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) – 7 marks**

1. **Problem Articulation (1 mark):** Define the problem, its boundary, and the time horizon. Example: *Forecast smartphone adoption in Kerala from 2024 to 2030.*

2. **Causal Loop Diagram (1 mark):** Sketch the CLD showing cause-effect relationships. Mark R and B loops.

3. **Stock-Flow Diagram (1.5 marks):** Convert the CLD into a formal SFD with stocks (rectangles) and flows (valves).

4. **Mathematical Formulation (1.5 marks):** Translate each flow into an algebraic or differential equation.

5. **Parameter Estimation (0.5 mark):** Use historical data, expert opinion, or regression to assign numerical values.

6. **Simulation (0.5 mark):** Run the model in Vensim/STELLA/Simulink with $\Delta t = 0.25$.

7. **Validation & Policy Analysis (1 mark):** Compare simulated output with real data; if mismatch ≥ 10%, iterate.

**Part (b) – 7 marks**

**Stock:** $N(t)$ = cumulative adopters (number of customers).
**Parameter:** $m$ = market potential, $p$ = innovation coefficient, $q$ = imitation coefficient.

**Governing ODE:**

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \left[p + q \cdot \frac{N(t)}{m}\right] \cdot \big[m - N(t)\big]$$

**Derivation (substitute $a = p+q$, expand, separate, integrate):**

$$\int \frac{dN}{(p + qN/m)(m - N)} = \int dt$$

After algebraic simplification (using partial fractions), the **closed-form solution** is:

$$N(t) = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \dfrac{q}{p}\,e^{-(p+q)t}}$$

**Inflection point** (set $\dfrac{d^2N}{dt^2}=0$):

$$N^* = \frac{m}{2} - \frac{m \cdot p}{2q} \quad \text{at time} \quad t^* = \frac{\ln(q/p)}{p+q}$$

**Numerical example (1 mark):** $m = 1{,}000{,}000$, $p = 0.02$, $q = 0.4$:

$$N^* = 500{,}000 - 25{,}000 = 475{,}000 \text{ adopters} \quad \text{at} \quad t^* \approx 7.65 \text{ years}$$

#### **QUESTION B – 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Build a **System Dynamics financial cash-flow model** for a startup company. Identify the stock, the inflows, the outflows, and the auxiliary variables. Write the governing differential equation and the Euler update rule. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A project requires an initial investment of **₹5,00,000** and generates the following cash inflows: Year 1 → ₹1,50,000, Year 2 → ₹2,00,000, Year 3 → ₹2,50,000. The discount rate is **10% per annum**. Compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** and the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**. Use System Dynamics interpretation to advise whether the project should be accepted. *(7 marks)*

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) – 7 marks**

- **Stock:** $C(t)$ = Cash balance of the company (₹).
- **Inflow:** Revenue $R(t) = p \cdot s(t)$, where $p$ = unit price, $s(t)$ = units sold/month.
- **Outflow:** Expenses $E(t) = c_f + c_v \cdot s(t)$, where $c_f$ = fixed cost, $c_v$ = variable cost/unit.
- **Auxiliary:** Profit $\Pi(t) = R(t) - E(t)$; Burn rate = $-dC/dt$ when $C$ is declining.

**ODE:**

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = R(t) - E(t)$$

**Euler update rule:**

$$C_{t+\Delta t} = C_t + \Delta t \cdot \big[R(t) - E(t)\big]$$

With a feedback loop linking $C(t) \to$ Marketing Budget $\to$ Sales $\to R(t)$ (R-loop) and a balancing loop $C(t) \to$ Cost Reduction $\to E(t)$ (B-loop).

**Part (b) – 7 marks**

**NPV Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\text{NPV} = -5{,}00{,}000 + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.10} + \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10^2} + \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.10^3}$$

Compute each discounted term:

$$\frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.10} = 1{,}36{,}363.64$$

$$\frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.21} = 1{,}65{,}289.26$$

$$\frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.331} = 1{,}87{,}828.70$$

Sum of discounted inflows = $1{,}36{,}363.64 + 1{,}65{,}289.26 + 1{,}87{,}828.70 = 4{,}89{,}481.60$

$$\text{NPV} = 4{,}89{,}481.60 - 5{,}00{,}000 = -10{,}518.40 \;\text{₹}$$

Since **NPV < 0**, the project is *not financially viable* at a 10% discount rate.

**IRR Calculation (3 marks):**
Solve for $r^*$ such that:

$$-5{,}00{,}000 + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{(1+r^*)} + \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{(1+r^*)^2} + \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{(1+r^*)^3} = 0$$

By trial (linear interpolation between $r = 9\%$ and $r = 10\%$):

At $r = 9\%$: NPV = $+8{,}869$ ₹ (approx).
At $r = 10\%$: NPV = $-10{,}518$ ₹.

$$\text{IRR} \approx 9\% + \frac{8869}{8869 + 10518} \times 1\% = 9\% + 0.46\% \approx 9.46\%$$

**System Dynamics Interpretation (1 mark):** IRR (9.46%) < hurdle rate (10%), so the project should be **rejected**. The cash stock $C(t)$ will deplete before cumulative inflow recovers the ₹5,00,000 outflow.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in System Dynamics questions:**
> 1. **Confusing stocks and flows (–2 marks)** – A stock *accumulates*; a flow is a *rate*. "Population" is a stock; "birth rate" is a flow.
> 2. **Forgetting to label loops (R or B) (–1 mark)** – KTU key explicitly requires loop polarity.
> 3. **Omitting the Euler update equation** – Even if the ODE is correct, the simulation step is incomplete without $S_{t+\Delta t} = S_t + \Delta t(I_t - O_t)$.
> 4. **No units stated** – Always include units (₹, persons, units) on the stock and flow definitions.
> 5. **Forgetting feedback structure** – A "model" without at least one feedback loop is a *static* equation, not a *System Dynamics* model. You will lose at least 3 marks.
> 6. **Wrong DT or wrong integration scheme** – Mention RK4 for non-linear; Euler is acceptable for linear.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- [ ] System Dynamics was invented by **Jay W. Forrester (MIT, 1956)**.
- [ ] Five primitives: **Stocks, Flows, Auxiliaries, Connectors, Constants**.
- [ ] Master equation: $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = I - O$; Euler form: $S_{t+\Delta t} = S_t + \Delta t(I - O)$.
- [ ] **R-loop** ⇒ exponential behaviour; **B-loop** ⇒ goal-seeking (S-curve) behaviour.
- [ ] **Logistic growth**: $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = rP(1 - P/K)$, carrying capacity $K$ in *persons*.
- [ ] **Exponential growth**: $N(t) = N_0 e^{rt}$, $r$ in *1/time*.
- [ ] **Bass Diffusion**: $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = \left(p + q\dfrac{N}{m}\right)(m - N)$; closed form uses $a = p+q$.
- [ ] **NPV** = $\sum \dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$; accept project if NPV > 0.
- [ ] **IRR** = discount rate that sets NPV to zero; accept project if IRR > hurdle rate.
- [ ] **Inventory balance**: $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = $ Production $-$ Sales.
- [ ] **First-order delay**: $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = (\text{Desired} - S)/\tau$.
- [ ] Always use **CLD → SFD → ODE → Simulate** workflow.
- [ ] Default **DT = 0.25**; recommended **RK4** integration.
- [ ] Software: **Vensim PLE, STELLA, MATLAB/Simulink, Powersim**.
- [ ] **Loop polarity** must be explicitly labelled (R or B) in every CLD.
- [ ] A model *without* a feedback loop is *not* a System Dynamics model.
- [ ] **Inflection point of Bass curve**: $N^* = \dfrac{m}{2} - \dfrac{mp}{2q}$.
- [ ] For Financial Analysis: stock = Cash, inflow = Revenue, outflow = Expenses.
- [ ] For Decision Making: the decision lever modifies a flow, not a stock directly.
